## PAGE 05432: Generic Table Wizard

- page_id: `243969650`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243969650/Generic+Table+Wizard
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:51:34.648+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Model analysis > Model Visualizer
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Editions

639875940

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Editions

639875942

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Editions

639875941

#### NOTE: Editions

Editions

This functionality is available in Standard, Professional, Architect, and Enterprise editions only.

A [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Generic table' space=''] allows you to display a list of selected elements and their properties in a single place.

The Generic Table Wizard helps you create a table when model elements and their relationships are already created and specified. You can select which elements (e.g., classes, packages, use cases, and relationships) will be included in a new table. You can also select properties of the selected element types, which will be displayed as columns in the table.

The Generic Table Wizard guides you through several steps and collects information along the way. It will automatically create a table with your selected elements, freeing you from creating the table manually.

To create a Generic Table using the Generic Table Wizard

1. Open the Generic Table Wizard by doing one of the following:
  - On the Diagrams menu, select Diagram Wizards > Generic Table Wizard .
  - From the Analyze menu, select Model Visualizer . In the open The Model Visualizer dialog, select to open the Generic Table Wizard and click Start .
2. Follow the steps as directed by the wizard.

639875939

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Generic table' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243971310 space=MT version=3 -->
## PAGE 05433: Getting a value from a MagicDraw element to a template

- page_id: `243971310`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243971310/Getting+a+value+from+a+MagicDraw+element+to+a+template
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-09-12T13:47:10.815+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Report Wizard > Template Creation Tutorial > Creating Your First Template
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1355429125

1355429128

1355429124

**On this page**

3

1355429128

#### Getting element values to a template

You can access the value of an element, and include it in a report in Report Wizard.

To get a value from a modeling tool element to a template

1. First you need to know the element type and attributes. Open the Specification dialog to see the type and attributes. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The Specification dialog showing the element type on the title bar and the attributes in the properties specification table.
2. Open Microsoft Word and type, for example: text The example above shows that **$Class** is an array that contains all the Class elements in a **Selected Element Scope** (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Report Wizard' space=''] for more information). **$class** is the individual Class element inside the array. To access the value of a property in **$class**, type: **$class** followed by **"."** and the attribute's name ("**.name**", "**.visibility**", and "**.isAbstract**" are the names of the **$class** attribute). The syntax for accessing the attribute value can be represented by: **$[Referenced object].[Attribute's name]**.

When you generate the output of the template for a project with a single Class element, the result will be shown as follows:

```text

```

#### Glossary

- Array An array is a container object that holds a fixed number of values of a single type. The length of an array is established when the array is created. After it is created, its length is fixed.
- Attribute Attribute defines values that can be attached to instances of the class or interface. For example, an attribute of a class represents the characteristics of the class.
- Class Class is the concrete realization of EncapsulatedClassifier and BehavioredClassifier. The purpose of a class is to specify a classification of objects, and to specify the Features that characterize the structure and behavior of those objects.
- Element Element is a constituent of a model. Every element has the inherent capability of owning other elements. When an element is removed from a model, all its ownedElements are also removed from the model. The abstract syntax for each kind of element specifies what other kind of elements it may own. Every element in a model must be owned by exactly one other element of that model, with the exception of the top-level Packages of the model.

#### More information about working with values in Report Wizard

To learn more about working with values in Report Wizard, visit the following pages:

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Retrieving Slot Values' space='']

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Getting property value' space='']

Open the Specification dialog to see the type and attributes.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="classspec.png" /></ac:image></p><h6>The Specification dialog showing the element type on the title bar and the attributes in the properties specification table.</h6></li><li><p>Open Microsoft Word and type, for example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="68018f45-ecc9-4f44-898d-539c24d3e41c"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach ($class in $Class)
````html
 Name: $class.name
 Owner: $class.owner.name
 Visibility: $class.visibility
 Is Abstract: $class.isAbstract
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The example above shows that <strong>$Class</strong> is an array that contains all the Class elements in a <strong>Selected Element Scope</strong> (see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Report Wizard" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Report Wizard user guide]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for more information). <strong>$class</strong> is the individual Class element inside the array. To access the value of a property in <strong>$class</strong>, type: <strong>$class</strong> followed by <strong>&quot;.&quot;</strong> and the attribute's name (&quot;<strong>.name</strong>&quot;, &quot;<strong>.visibility</strong>&quot;, and &quot;<strong>.isAbstract</strong>&quot; are the names of the <strong>$class</strong> attribute). The syntax for accessing the attribute value can be represented by: <strong>$[Referenced object].[Attribute's name]</strong>. </p></li></ol><p>When you generate the output of the template for a project with a single Class element, the result will be shown as follows:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8a810927-61b0-4b82-8bd9-273ff4b33faa"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Name: Customer
 Owner: com
 Visibility: public
 Is Abstract: false]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h2>Glossary</h2><ul><li><strong>Array</strong><br />An array <span style="color: rgb(84,84,84);">is a container object that holds a fixed number of values of a single type. The length of an </span><span style="color: rgb(106,106,106);">array</span><span style="color: rgb(84,84,84);"> is established when the </span><span style="color: rgb(106,106,106);">array</span><span style="color: rgb(84,84,84);"> is created. After it is created, its length is fixed.</span></li><li><strong>Attribute</strong><br />Attribute defines values that can be attached to instances of the class or interface. For example, an attribute of a class represents the characteristics of the class.</li><li class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Class</strong><br />Class is the concrete realization of EncapsulatedClassifier and BehavioredClassifier. The purpose of a class is to specify a classification of objects, and to specify the Features that characterize the structure and behavior of those objects.</li><li class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Element</strong><br />Element is a constituent of a model. Every element has the inherent capability of owning other elements. When an element is removed from a model, all its ownedElements are also removed from the model. The abstract syntax for each kind of element specifies what other kind of elements it may own. Every element in a model must be owned by exactly one other element of that model, with the exception of the top-level Packages of the model.</li></ul><h2>More information about working with values in Report Wizard</h2><p>To learn more about working with values in Report Wizard, visit the following pages:</p><p><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Retrieving Slot Values" /></ac:link></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Getting property value" /></ac:link></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243969426 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05434: Getting comparison summary information and changes legend

- page_id: `243969426`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243969426/Getting+comparison+summary+information+and+changes+legend
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:51:28.804+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Model analysis > Comparing projects > Analyzing comparison results
- labels: ['summary-and-legend', 'comparison-summary', 'comparison-result', 'msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can see how many differences have been detected between the base and compared project version on the **Summary and legend** panel that is located at the top right of the **Difference Viewer** window. The panel also helps to understand the decorations and highlighting of elements in both the **Differences**tree and the **Specification** panel.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>You can see how many differences have been detected between the base and compared project version on the <strong>Summary and legend</strong> panel that is located at the top right of the <strong>Difference Viewer</strong> window. The panel also helps to understand the decorations and highlighting of elements in both the <strong>Differences </strong>tree and the <strong> <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="fa21c6bb-741a-4318-83ff-bf732f4ae486">Specification</ac:inline-comment-marker> </strong> <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="fa21c6bb-741a-4318-83ff-bf732f4ae486"> panel.</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:border="true" ac:title="Project comparison summary and legend" ac:alt="Project comparison summary and legend"><ri:attachment ri:filename="compare_legend.png" /></ac:image></p><p> </p>
````

- page_id: `243968576`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243968576/Getting+merge+summary+information+and+changes+legend

- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:51:03.083+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Tools > Model Merge > Analyzing and managing merge results
- labels: ['merge-summary', 'summary-and-legend', 'merged-result', 'merge-legend', 'msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1767089336

INLINE

1767089338

INLINE

1767089337

INLINE

The **Summary/Legend** panel is located at the bottom left of the **Merge** window. The panel helps to understand the marking and highlighting of elements in both the **Merged Result** tree/list and the **Specification** panel.

On the **Summary/Legend** panel, for 3-way merge, you can see how many differences have been detected between the ancestor and the source, as well as between the ancestor and the target.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

For 2-way merge, you can see how many differences have been detected between two versions, and what kind of changes were detected.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

1767089335

INLINE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243970782 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05436: Getting property value

- page_id: `243970782`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243970782/Getting+property+value
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:51:58.083+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Report Wizard > Template variables
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can directly get the property value of the element by calling property name:

```text

```

**propertyName** should be typed in camel case style. The name and returned type of property can be found in UMLMetaModel UserGuide. It lists all the properties which can be called through each element type.

Please be aware of the type of the returned value, as some properties may return an array. You can get each value by using a **#foreach** directive or by specifying array index number. For example:

```text

```

```text

```

Moreover, the customized elements are able to get their property value immediately by calling property name:

```text

```

You can see the representation text of the element by opening the Specification dialog.

The dialog name pattern is

```text
]]>
```

While the property name is shown in the same dialog.

If the property value is not printed out to your report, you can assume the property that you call is not the property of the customized element. It may be the property of the applied stereotype. You would rather check the stereotype owner of that property by going to the Applied Stereotype property of the element, then right click on the right column, and select the stereotype in the containment tree.

If you navigate to the stereotype element on the containment tree, then you can expand it to see its owned properties. If you can see your desired property in the stereotype, you can get its value by:

```text

```

We also provide helper module in section *[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Helper tools' space='']*. These modules are utility modules for getting property values. More details about openAPI of each element can be found at:

*{md_install}\plugins\com.nomagic.magicdraw.reportwizard\api\javadoc.zip*

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

The name and returned type of property can be found in UMLMetaModel UserGuide. It lists all the properties which can be called through each element type.</p><p>Please be aware of the type of the returned value, as some properties may return an array. You can get each value by using a <strong>#foreach</strong> directive or by specifying array index number. For example:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c63d14e2-10d4-42d2-9839-cfdb8d143030"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach($c in $class.classifier)
$c.name
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="02b425b7-c111-4c29-a335-f9603f84261e"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#set($classifier = $class.classifier)
$classifier.get(0)
$classifier.get(1)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Moreover, the customized elements are able to get their property value immediately by calling property name:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="817fc16a-028f-4179-8361-0ddafae72d98"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$RepresentationText.propertyName]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>You can see the representation text of the element by opening the Specification dialog.</p><p>The dialog name pattern is </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b36348c4-070a-46ff-9aab-a36d797f7a4c"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Specification of <RepresentationText> <ElementName>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>While the property name is shown in the same dialog.</p><p>If the property value is not printed out to your report, you can assume the property that you call is not the property of the customized element. It may be the property of the applied stereotype. You would rather check the stereotype owner of that property by going to the Applied Stereotype property of the element, then right click on the right column, and select the stereotype in the containment tree.</p><p>If you navigate to the stereotype element on the containment tree, then you can expand it to see its owned properties. If you can see your desired property in the stereotype, you can get its value by:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="af6d8949-f382-44c9-bb2d-ebd943467c0e"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$report.getStereotypePropertyString($element, "stereotypeName", "propertyName")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br />We also provide helper module in section <em><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Helper tools" /></ac:link></em>. These modules are utility modules for getting property values. More details about openAPI of each element can be found at:</p><p><em>{md_install}\plugins\com.nomagic.magicdraw.reportwizard\api\javadoc.zip</em></p>
````

## PAGE 05437: Getting started

- page_id: `243965702`

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243968968 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05438: Getting started with specifying criteria

- page_id: `243968968`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243968968/Getting+started+with+specifying+criteria
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-07T17:23:49.970+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Model analysis > Specifying criteria for querying model
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

2217659847

2217659849

1759777648

**On this page:**

3

2217659848

##### Opening the dialog for specifying criteria

The dialog for specifying criteria can be opened from several different places.

To open the dialog for specifying criteria, do one of the following

- In the Criteria area of a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Dependency Matrix' space=''] , click the ... button next to the Dependency Criteria box. The Dependency Criteria dialog opens.
- In the Criteria area of a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Relation Map' space=''] , click the ... button next to the Relation Criteria box. The Relation Critera dialog opens.
- Open the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space=''] of a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Smart Package' space='MED'] , under the Content category on the right, click the cell of the Query property value, and then click the ... button. The Query dialog opens.
- Open the Specification window of a selected element, click [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Traceability' space=''] on the left, and then click Create on the right. The Expression dialog opens.
- On the toolbar of a generic table, click the Columns button, and then select [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom traceability rules' space=''] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with columns' space=''] . The Expression dialog opens.
- Open the Specification window of an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating executable opaque behaviors' space=''] , click the cell of the Body and Language property value, and then click the ... button. When the Body and Language dialog opens, select StructuredExpression from the Language drop-down list.
- Open the Specification window of a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating and managing Legend Items' space=''] , click the specification cell of the Elements by Condition property, and then click [ATTACHMENT filename='edit.png'] . The Elements by Condition dialog opens.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The layout of the dialog is the same in all the described cases. On the left side of the dialog, you can select an operation type that best suits for specifying the needed criterion. Selecting an operation type opens the criteria specification panel on the right side of the dialog.

##### Specified and unspecified operation display

The Simple Navigation, Metachain Navigation, Find, and Implied Relation operations are always displayed on the left side of the dialog, even if they were removed during the previous edits of the expression. For this reason, empty template operation nodes are displayed in the italic format below the specified operations. Once you specify an expression for a template operation, a template operation of that type will not appear during subsequent edits. In such a case, only the specified operation is displayed. You can create a new operation via *Create operation...* option.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']On the left: empty template operations in italic format. 
On the right: template operations with specified operations in roman format. Empty template operations of these types no longer appear during the subsequent edit.

If an operation is specified, it is displayed in roman format and at the top of the operations list. You can manually rearrange the specified operations by dragging and dropping them anywhere on the list. In such cases, the arrangement of the specified operations stays the same when you open the expression again.

Also, be advised that before starting to specify criteria it is useful to read the tips and warnings (if there are any), which appear after the pointer is hovered over the [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button or the [IMAGE alt='' src='']button respectively.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
'Find' operation is specified, appearing in roman formatand at the top of the operations list. Other operations under 'Find' are not specified and are thus italicized.

2217659846

  - Using Metachain Navigation
  - Using Find operation
  - Using Implied Relations

    - Calling operations from the model
    - Creating script operations

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=246156566 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05439: Global options for Code Engineering

- page_id: `246156566`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/246156566/Global+options+for+Code+Engineering
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-07T17:24:40.733+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Code engineering
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Code Engineering Options for all Sets in your Project

Choose**Project**from the **Options**menu. TheProject Optionsdialog box appears.You can set the Code Engineering options in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Setting project options' space='']**Project Options** dialog. The **Project Options** dialog box has two main collections of customizable options, represented by the hierarchy tree on the left side of the dialog box. The figure below illustrates **Code****Engineering-**related options.

In the option list, select **Code engineering**and specify the following items:

| Item name | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Default Working Directory | Enter the name of your working directory. |
| Default Working Package | Define any package for the reverse output or code generation. Your model will be reversed or the code will be generated from this specified package. |
| Default Paths for References | Add specific profiles, used projects, or libraries to define where to search for paths for references during reverse/code generation. |
| Default Encoding | Click to open a list of available encodings. |
| Default language | When selected, the syntax checker of the selected language runs while Code Engineering is executed. |
| Use Syntax Checker | When selected, the syntax checker runs while Code Engineering is executed. |
| Directory for Temporal Files | You can specify Active Directory, System, or you can select another directory. |

The following table describes other **Code Engineering**options:

| Option | Element Name | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Code Generation |  | Set code generation options using the fields listed on the right side of the Project options dialog box. The Code generation area contains boxes with the same functionality as the Code generations options dialog box. See . |
| Reverse |  | Set reverse options for all reverse actions of the project using the options listed on the right side of the Project options dialog box. The area contains boxes that have the same functionality as in the Reverse options dialog box. |
| DDL Language Options | Generate opening bracket in new line | Opens a bracket in the newly generated line. |
|  | Generate spaces | Spaces inside an assignment and other operators are generated. |
|  | Generate documentation | Comment brackets are placed in your code, unless the class in the model has no documentation. |
|  | Header | Add the specific header to all your code files. Click the button and enter header text in the Header dialog. You may also define $DATE, $AUTHOR, and $TIME in the header. |

**Related Pages**:

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Code engineering' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

The <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Reverse Options" /></ac:link> area contains boxes that have the same functionality as in the Reverse options dialog box.</span></td></tr><tr class=""><td><strong>DDL Language Options</strong></td><td><strong>Generate opening bracket in new line</strong></td><td><p><span>Opens a bracket in the newly generated line.</span></p></td></tr><tr class=""><td><br /></td><td><strong>Generate spaces </strong></td><td><p><span>Spaces inside an assignment and other operators are generated.</span></p></td></tr><tr class=""><td><br /></td><td><strong>Generate documentation </strong></td><td><p><span>Comment brackets are placed in your code, unless the class in the model has no documentation. </span></p></td></tr><tr class=""><td><br /></td><td><p><strong>Header </strong></p></td><td><div class="content-wrapper"><p><span>Add the specific header to all your code files. Click the <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="22"><ri:attachment ri:filename="three-dot-btn1.png" /></ac:image> button and enter header text in the <strong>Header</strong> dialog. You may also define $DATE, $AUTHOR, and $TIME in the header.</span></p></div></td></tr></tbody></table><p><br /></p><p><strong>Related Pages</strong>:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="490faf13-3e88-47fe-b7f7-5fb0aa644afe" /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="02d35c92-f5cd-436c-8780-8041a4ea0520"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Code engineering" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243969607 space=MT version=3 -->
## PAGE 05440: Global validation rules

- page_id: `243969607`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243969607/Global+validation+rules
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-09-12T13:28:22.483+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Model analysis > Validation > Custom validation > Creating new validation rules
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### EXCERPT: Sample model

INLINE

Global validation rules check the result of an element set, while validation rules contained in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Predefined validation suites' space=''] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Active validation suites' space=''] are dedicated to check a separate element. You can create global validation rules in the same way as other validation rules by defining an appropriate condition in the**Specification** property.

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating new validation rules' space='']]]>
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Defining Constraint condition' space='']>]]>

For example, the *balanced budget* validation rule (Constraint specification is shown in the following figure) is a global validation rule. It is created for the classifier of the *ReseachProject* Class. The *balanced budget* validation rule condition, defined by using OCL2.0 language in the **Specification** property, expresses that the sum of the expenses of all projects must be less than the sum of all *Sponsor* contributions. This validation rule is global because it includes all instances in the model, rather than only the particular instance of *ResearchProject* that violates the rule.

#### TIP: Sample model

Sample model

An example of a global validation rule can be found in the **model validation** sample model > *Classifiers Validation Rules* [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class diagram' space='']. You can find this sample at*<modeling tool installation directory>\samples\product features\model validation.mdzip.*

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The specification of the global validation rule named balanced budget.

After the validation finishes, the global validation rule is marked as <model> in the****[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Validation Results panel' space='']**Validation Results** panel > **Element** column. This means that the entire model, not the separate element, violates the rule.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The representation of global validation rule in the Validation Results panel.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="excerpt" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8e7d2804-a264-40ce-926d-7a481831cffb"><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Global validation rules check the result of an element set, while validation rules contained in <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Predefined validation suites" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[predefined validation suites]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> or <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Active validation suites" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[active validation suites]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> are dedicated to check a separate element. You can create global validation rules in the same way as other validation rules by defining an appropriate condition in the<strong> Specification</strong> property.</p><ul><li><span class="confluence-link"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating new validation rules" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[How to create validation rules >]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span></li><li><span class="confluence-link"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Defining Constraint condition" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[How to define Constraint expression >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span></li></ul><p><span class="confluence-link">For example, the <em class="confluence-link">balanced budget</em> validation rule (Constraint specification is shown in the following figure) is a global validation rule. It is created for the classifier of the <em>ReseachProject</em> Class. The <em>balanced budget</em> validation rule condition, defined by using OCL2.0 language in the <strong>Specification</strong> property, expresses that the <span class="confluence-link">sum of the expenses of all projects must be less than the sum of all <em>Sponsor</em> contributions.</span> This validation rule is global because <span class="confluence-link">it includes all instances in the model, rather than only the particular instance of <em>ResearchProject</em> that violates the rule.</span></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e8d02d80-9013-40b3-a6ca-84b3304847c4"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Sample model</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>An example of a global validation rule can be found in the <strong>model validation</strong> sample model &gt; <em>Classifiers Validation Rules</em> <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Class diagram" /></ac:link>. You can find this sample at<span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> </span><em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;\<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">samples\product features</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">\<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">model validation</span>.mdzip.</span></span></em></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span class="confluence-link"> </span></p><p><span class="confluence-link"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="global_validation_rule.png" /></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span class="confluence-link"><span class="confluence-link">The specification of the global validation rule named <span class="confluence-link">balanced budget.</span><br /></span></span></h6><p><br /></p><p>After the validation finishes, the global validation rule is marked as &lt;model&gt; in the<strong> </strong><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Validation Results panel" /><ac:link-body><strong>Validation Results</strong> panel</ac:link-body></ac:link> &gt; <strong>Element</strong> column. This means that the entire model, not the separate element, violates the rule.</p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="global_validation_rule_in_validation_results_panel.png" /></ac:image><br />The representation of global validation rule in the Validation Results panel.</h6><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

## PAGE 05441: Glossary table

- page_id: `243966961`
- space_key: `MT`

**On this page**

219319400

##### **Description**

The Glossary saves time by ensuring consistent usage of terminology in the organization. It improves communication between team members, as terms are understood to have the same definitions and usages everywhere they appear.

Each row in the table represents a term. A word, phrase, or any element of the model can be a term. Words and phrases defined as terms are underlined. This enables you to identify terms in your model.

The word or phrase is underlined only if it completely matches the defined term. For example, if you defined the word *Project* in your glossary, the word *Projects* would not be underlined because it is plural.

##### **Purpose**

The Glossary Table allows you to:

- Create a list of all the specific terms used in the project.
- Define your terms in one place.
- Use them everywhere in your project.
- Export your glossary to a new project and use it in other projects.
- Export your glossary into .html, .csv, or .xlsx files.
- Have several glossaries in one project.
- Customize the representation of the glossary in the table.

##### **Usage**

The video below demonstrates how to use the glossary when working with Requirements.

1000550

##### **Example**

The following figure illustrates the terms defined in the Glossary Table.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The figure below shows the term visibility on the diagram pane. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### **Specific tasks in the Glossary Table**

The following outlines the specific procedures when working with Glossary Tables:

## PAGE 05442: Goal

- page_id: `249566836`
- space_key: `MT`

- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > BPMN modeling > Supportive Diagrams Concepts > Business Motivation Diagram > Ends Concepts

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1487132151

1487132152

1487132152

**Description**

Unlike vision, a goal should generally be attainable and should be more specifically oriented to a single aspect of the business problem. A Goal is a statement about a state or condition of the enterprise to be brought about or sustained through appropriate Means. A Goal amplifies a Vision.It indicates what must be satisfied on a continuing basis to effectively attain the Vision.

**Notation**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

1487132150

- End Concept Relationships

## PAGE 05444: Groovy script tool

- page_id: `243971210`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243971210/Groovy+script+tool
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:52:08.533+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Report Wizard
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1357810331

1357810332

1357810332

Groovy Script tool enables report templates to evaluate or run Groovy codes from templates and external Groovy files.

The Groovy tool includes two methods:

**(i) 'eval' method** 
 This method will evaluate Groovy text, and then return the result. 
**(ii) 'execute' method** 
 This method will execute a Groovy file, and then return the result.

Like other Script Tools, the Groovy Script Tool '*scripttool.jar*' must be installed in the 'extensions' folder of the Report Wizard plugin. The same '*groovy-all-1.7.9.jar*' of the Automaton plugin is used, so make sure the Automaton plugin is enabled and loaded. For further information about Custom Tools and the installation, see *[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Custom tool' space='']*.

To import Groovy Script tool to a template, type the following code:

```text

```

1357810330

**Related pages**

The same '<em>groovy-all-1.7.9.jar</em>' of the Automaton plugin is used, so make sure the Automaton plugin is enabled and loaded. For further information about Custom Tools and the installation, see <em><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Custom tool" /></ac:link></em>.</p><p>To import Groovy Script tool to a template, type the following code:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d52fdb3f-26d9-4509-9180-f02fbf465806"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#import ('groovy', 'com.nomagic.reportwizard.tools.script.GroovyTool')]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7c0f5b2f-4439-4633-99c9-eeb069610521"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1357810330</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="987fab6b-993d-42a2-a0f9-7a7759bd6216" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243971211 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05445: Groovy script tool API

- page_id: `243971211`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243971211/Groovy+script+tool+API
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:52:08.581+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Report Wizard > Groovy script tool
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### 'eval' Method

**eval(String script)**

This method will evaluate a Groovy code from a string and return the result.

```text

```

**eval(String script, String bindingName, Object bindingObject)**

This method will evaluate a Groovy code with a single binding object and specified binding name. The code will be evaluated from a string. The binding name will be used as the name for this object.

```text

```

**eval(String script, Map bindingMap)**

This method will evaluate a Groovy code with a set of binding arguments (a name and an object). The code will be evaluated from a string. The binding map consists of key-value pairs for the binding name and the binding object.

```text

```

Or

```text

```

The second code contains curly brackets; '{' and '}' characters, which are not allowed to be used in any *RTF* template. For the *RTF* template, use the first code instead.

#### 'execute' method

**execute(String filename)**

This method will execute a Groovy file. The 'filename' parameter refers to a name of the Groovy file, an absolute path to the Groovy file, or a relative path starting from the template to the Groovy file.

```text

```

If the Groovy file contains Groovy functions, you can recall the functions by using 'eval()' methods.

```text

```

**execute(String filename, String bindingName, Object bindingObject)**

This method will execute a Groovy file with a single binding object and specified binding name. The 'filename' parameter refers to a name of the Groovy file, an absolute path to the Groovy file, or a relative path starting from the template to the Groovy file. The binding name will be used as the name for this object.

File filename.groovy

```text

```

The template code

```text

```

**execute(String filename, Map bindingMap)**

This method will execute a Groovy file with a set of binding arguments (a name and an object). The 'filename' parameter refers to a name of the Groovy file, an absolute path to the Groovy file, or a relative path starting from the template to the Groovy file. The binding map consists of key-value pairs for the binding name and the binding object.

File filename.groovy

```text

```

The template code

```text

```

- Absolute Path : If the 'filename' is provided with an absolute path, Groovy Tool will read the Groovy file from an absolute location such as $groovy.execute(' c:/mycode/readclass.groovy ') .
- Relative Path : If the 'filename' is provided with a relative path, Groovy Tool will read the template from a relative location. This relative location starts from the current directory in which the template is located such as $groovy.execute(' readclass.groovy ') .

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<h2>'eval' Method</h2><p><strong>eval(String script)</strong></p><p>This method will evaluate a Groovy code from a string and return the result.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5a33a1f1-f29e-4307-81b9-57862248176f"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$groovy.eval("println 'Hello World!'")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><strong>eval(String script, String bindingName, Object bindingObject)</strong></p><p>This method will evaluate a Groovy code with a single binding object and specified binding name. The code will be evaluated from a string. The binding name will be used as the name for this object.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="58fe7cf5-28c9-4058-8c49-b196e383723f"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach ($c in $Class)
$groovy.eval("println classname", "classname", $c.name)
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><strong>eval(String script, Map bindingMap)</strong></p><p>This method will evaluate a Groovy code with a set of binding arguments (a name and an object). The code will be evaluated from a string. The binding map consists of key-value pairs for the binding name and the binding object.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c9f6d2fe-35b7-4026-ab7c-eddd9e604484"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#set ($dict = $map.createHashMap())
#set ($void = $dict.put("first", "foo"))
#set ($void = $dict.put("last", "bar"))
$groovy.eval("println first + last", $dict)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Or</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="78fb6923-74a4-42f2-80ed-7dd51974bb4a"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$groovy.eval("println first + ' ' + last", {"first":"foo", "last":"bar"})]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="65cfc62e-bc62-457d-9602-34b93ca8b902"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The second code contains curly brackets; '{' and '}' characters, which are not allowed to be used in any <em>RTF</em> template. For the <em>RTF</em> template, use the first code instead.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h2>'execute' method</h2><p><strong>execute(String filename)</strong></p><p>This method will execute a Groovy file. The 'filename' parameter refers to a name of the Groovy file, an absolute path to the Groovy file, or a relative path starting from the template to the Groovy file.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4c45cd41-14fe-49c2-b655-ba8d72fabcca"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$groovy.execute("filename.groovy")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>If the Groovy file contains Groovy functions, you can recall the functions by using 'eval()' methods.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="23471ea8-420f-4626-ac33-ceee8da6a8e4"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$groovy.execute('filename.groovy')
$groovy.eval("new groovy().functionname()")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><strong>execute(String filename, String bindingName, Object bindingObject)</strong></p><p>This method will execute a Groovy file with a single binding object and specified binding name. The 'filename' parameter refers to a name of the Groovy file, an absolute path to the Groovy file, or a relative path starting from the template to the Groovy file. The binding name will be used as the name for this object.</p><p>File filename.groovy</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8419f1c5-de5f-440f-ae12-f0bad450ecdc"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA["Class name is " + $c.name]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The template code</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1977f794-8fcd-4669-8b9c-2dcab9cdb9be"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach ($c in $Class)
$groovy.execute("filename.groovy", 'c', $c)

The binding map consists of key-value pairs for the binding name and the binding object.</p><p>File filename.groovy</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="be28b4ed-e9b6-4192-a2e9-286c4e728837"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[first + " " + last]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The template code</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cfecb802-5ca5-4f91-a3e4-78cec3eeb108"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#set ($dict = $map.createHashMap())
#set ($void = $dict.put("first", "foo"))
#set ($void = $dict.put("last", "bar"))
$groovy.execute("filename.groovy", $dict)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7e460a72-333c-46ee-b0de-6d1efa48f9c6"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><em>Absolute Path</em>: If the 'filename' is provided with an absolute path, Groovy Tool will read the Groovy file from an absolute location such as <strong>$groovy.execute('</strong><em>c:/mycode/readclass.groovy</em><strong>')</strong>.</li><li><em>Relative Path</em>: If the 'filename' is provided with a relative path, Groovy Tool will read the template from a relative location. This relative location starts from the current directory in which the template is located such as <strong>$groovy.execute('</strong><em>readclass.groovy</em><strong>')</strong>.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

## PAGE 05446: Group

- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

148778302

148778302

148778302

**Description**

A Group element represents an informal visual grouping of the diagram graphical elements.

A group shows all elements that belong to the same category. This type of grouping does not affect a sequence flow within the Group. A category name appears on the diagram as a Group label.

**Example**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

148778301

**Related diagrams**

- BPMN Process Diagram
- BPMN Collaboration Diagram
- BPMN Choreography Diagram

###### **$group.create()**

###### **$group.init()**

| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Parameter(s) | - | - | - |
| Return | - | void | - |

****

###### **$group.groupNames()**

****

###### **$group.contains(groupName)**

Test whether a group name is contained in a set of group names.

|  | Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Parameter(s) | groupName | java.lang.String | A group name. |
| Return | - | - | True if the group name is contained in a set of group names, otherwise false. |

****

###### **$group.put(groupName, object)**

Add an object into a group.

|  | Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Parameter(s) | groupName | java.lang.String | A group name. |
|  | object | java.lang.Object | An object being added. |

|  | Name | Type | Description |

| Parameter(s) | groupName | java.lang.String | A group name. |

###### **$group.remove(groupName)**

|  | Name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249567560 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05448: Groups

- page_id: `249567560`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249567560/Groups
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-18T18:31:02.965+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > Data-related modeling > XML schemas > XML schema mapping to UML elements
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Maps to UML Class with the stereotype XSDgroup.

This class may also have the stereotype XSDall, XSDsequence or XSDchoice.

If a group has the ref attribute, the group definition maps to the UML Attribute or the UML Association End. The UML Attribute must have XSDgroupRef**stereotype. This stereotype may be omitted for the AssociationEnd.

#### PANEL: group XML representation summary

group XML representation summary

```text
<group
```

```text
name = NCName>
```

```text
Content: (annotation?, (all | choice | sequence))
```

```text
</group><group
```

```text
ref = OName
```

```text
maxOccurs = (nonNegativeInteger | unbounded) : 1
```

```text
minOccurs = nonNegativeInteger : 1>
```

```text
Content: (annotation?)
```

```text
</group>
```

#### PANEL: group XML code sample

group XML code sample

```text
<xs:group name="myModelGroup">	<xs:sequence>
```

```text
<xs:element ref="someThing"/>		...
```

```text
</xs:sequence></xs:group>
```

```text
<xs:complexType name="trivial">	<xs:group ref="myModelGroup"/>	<xs:attribute…/></xs:complexType>
```

```text
<xs:complexType name="moreSo">	<xs:choice>
```

```text
<xs:element ref="anotherThing"/>		<xs:group ref="myModelGroup"/>	</xs:choice>
```

```text
<xs:attribute…/></xs:complexType>
```

For group UML model example, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Compositors' space=''].

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

The UML Attribute must have XSDgroupRef<em> </em>stereotype. This stereotype may be omitted for the AssociationEnd.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d765330c-9614-46b3-98c1-df2e4339d7d8"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">group XML representation summary</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><pre>&lt;group</pre><pre>	name = NCName&gt;</pre><pre>	Content: (annotation?, (all | choice | sequence))</pre><pre>&lt;/group&gt;<br />&lt;group</pre><pre>	ref = OName</pre><pre>	maxOccurs = (nonNegativeInteger | unbounded) : 1</pre><pre>	minOccurs = nonNegativeInteger : 1&gt;</pre><pre>  	Content: (annotation?)</pre><pre>&lt;/group&gt;</pre></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e9b73bb3-40ee-494f-9bea-ced795054a0d"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">group XML code sample</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><pre>&lt;xs:group name=&quot;myModelGroup&quot;&gt;<br />	&lt;xs:sequence&gt;</pre><pre>		&lt;xs:element ref=&quot;someThing&quot;/&gt;<br />		...</pre><pre>	&lt;/xs:sequence&gt;<br />&lt;/xs:group&gt;<br /><br /></pre><pre>&lt;xs:complexType name=&quot;trivial&quot;&gt;<br />	&lt;xs:group ref=&quot;myModelGroup&quot;/&gt;<br />	&lt;xs:attribute…/&gt;<br />&lt;/xs:complexType&gt;<br /><br /></pre><pre>&lt;xs:complexType name=&quot;moreSo&quot;&gt;<br />	&lt;xs:choice&gt;</pre><pre>		&lt;xs:element ref=&quot;anotherThing&quot;/&gt;<br />		&lt;xs:group ref=&quot;myModelGroup&quot;/&gt;<br />	&lt;/xs:choice&gt;</pre><pre>	&lt;xs:attribute…/&gt;<br />&lt;/xs:complexType&gt;</pre></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>For group UML model example, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Compositors" /></ac:link>.</p>
