<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243973876 space=MT version=7 -->
## PAGE 05449: Guidelines for working with large models

- page_id: `243973876`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243973876/Guidelines+for+working+with+large+models
- version_number: 7
- version_date: `2025-09-11T19:26:55.781+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Product performance can be highly dependent on the model organization strategy. From long-term work experience with various clients, we have selected the biggest challenges and gathered their possible solutions in one place.

##### Teamwork Cloud Operations SpecificsTeamwork Cloud Operations Specifics

###### Project Opening

Opening Teamwork Cloud project for the first time may take a bit longer, as all project-related data has to be downloaded from the server and then processed in the modeling client. The download time depends on the project size and network speed, e.g., for large projects (containing 5M/3.5M model elements in "19.0 and before/2021x and later" version), it may require downloading 1GB or more of data. Once the project is opened, we store your project data locally. The processing time depends on the downloaded project size. Opening the project for the second time is much faster as there is no need to request all data from the server again. Instead, the previously downloaded and locally stored data is reused. Only new information has to be downloaded from the server. The maximum processed data count is specified by the **Recent Files List Size** environment option. The processed project data is always preserved when the project is saved offline. 
More information can be found [here](https://docs.nomagic.com/CM/?contextKey=opening-projects-from-teamwork-cloud&version=latest). 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Open Server Project dialog.

###### Committing

Frequent commits make it easier for other team members to track project changes; however, this should be avoided if many users are working on a particular project. Frequent commits create a lot of project versions and increase the commit time due to the need to update the project every time. You need to decide upon the best-committing strategy within your company. A widely used practice is to commit the changes at the end of the work day; however, this should be avoided because, in some cases, the project commit may take longer than expected. 
More information can be found [here](https://docs.nomagic.com/CM/?contextKey=committing-changes-to-teamwork-cloud&version=latest).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Switching Used Project versions

Teamwork Cloud projects are usually built from smaller parts. Different teams simultaneously develop different parts of the project, so there is a need to keep the model up-to-date. A user is notified when a new version of the used project arrives, and it is up to the user whether to apply the update or not. As not all used project version updates are important, there is no need to update the version each time a new version arrives. Therefore, it is best to set up a version update strategy within the teams. You can decide whether or not to switch to a new used project version by considering the severity of that version – the commit tags can be used to mark that the particular version contains the major changes and should be used instead. 
More information can be found [here](https://docs.nomagic.com/CM/?contextKey=changing-used-project-versions&version=latest).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Notification about version changes.

In some cases, the project version update may take longer than expected due to a local cache update.

**Used Projects Auto Update Plugin**. This plugin allows users to set up specific used projects to update. We recommend that you schedule it to launch at night so that the latest versions will be ready to use the next work day. More information can be found [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Used Projects Auto Updater Plugin' space=''].

###### Merge

To perform a merge operation, the ancestor and contributor (source and target) projects have to be loaded, meaning that the memory consumption increases at least two times compared to the memory required to perform daily tasks in the project. After testing the memory consumption with different-sized projects, the following minimum memory requirement for the merge operation was identified: *2 x (memory required to load project) + 1 GB** .*These memory requirements are indicative and may vary by project. It strongly depends on the number of detected changes between merged versions (in this case, the project merge was done with 1.5k changes in total).

Since the merge operation uses a high amount of memory, please make sure that your machine is capable of performing this task. If the required memory exceeds the maximum amount of memory that can be allocated to the tool, the dedicated hardware should be used instead. In case of excessive memory usage, the merge operation can be optimized for lower memory consumption by excluding differing diagrams/elements count (**Optimize for** > **Memory** in the **Merge Projects** dialog); however, this optimization slows down the merge operation. 
More information can be found [here](https://docs.nomagic.com/CM/?contextKey=model-merge-in-teamwork-cloud&version=latest).

###### Project Decomposition

Model partitioning enables a more granular model management. Despite the benefits of granularity, project decomposition should be done carefully since managing used projects can become a bottleneck from a performance perspective. Please follow the criteria below to decompose a project:

- 20+ active users work on the same project simultaneously. As a result, the project contains many project versions, which slow down project history-related operations. Also, a higher number of users leads to a higher number of commits, resulting in longer update times and slowing down the commit operation.
- A large number of models owned by the same person indicates that the model granularity is too small or there is a need to distribute responsibilities.
- The part of the project (such as type libraries) should be used in other projects.
- Different teams work on a certain aspect of the system separately. All these separate parts are then connected in the integration project.

###### Publishing projects to Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud

As publishing greatly depends on the element count and diagram complexity (e.g., diagram type, the number of elements displayed, dynamic or static content, etc.), please consider the publishing scope before publishing. Since publishing uses a high amount of memory, please make sure that your machine is capable of performing this task. Remember that if the required memory exceeds the maximum amount of memory that can be allocated to the tool, the dedicated hardware should be used instead. 
More information can be found [here](https://docs.nomagic.com/MCS/?contextKey=publishing-documents&version=latest&variant=Teamwork%20Cloud%20and%20Services).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Publishing a model is the first step in the workflow for reviewing models and providing feedback.

###### Content History

If retrieving a particular element history takes a lot of time, it implies that the Content History action should be performed carefully as it retrieves the history of all contained elements at once. Before invoking this action, you should carefully consider the scope of the elements contained by the element for which the action is invoked. 
More information can be found [here](https://docs.nomagic.com/CM/?contextKey=reviewing-content-history&version=latest).[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### History of element changes in the scope of a *Book*package.

##### Teamwork Cloud Operations Troubleshooting

**Waiting for Response**

If the operation that requires a remote call to Teamwork Cloud is taking too long, you can analyze a *.log* file to learn how much time was spent processing the operation on the client and server-side.

Where do I find a .*log* file?

1. Open your modeling tool.
2. In the main menu, go to Help > About <modeling tool name> .
3. In the Environment tab, click the file path next to Log File to open a *.log* file.

**Measuring Network Latency**

*INFO NetworkPerformance - <operation name> latency <latency time>*

**Description**

The latency (i.e., the time it takes for the ping signal sent from the tool to the server to return) is measured for the following operations:

- Login
- Committing project to server
- Updating project
- Opening project

Example: INFO NetworkPerformance - Login latency 1 ms

**Remote Operations Duration Statistics**

*INFO RemoteCallsAnalyzer - <operation name> all operations time <total operation time>*

*INFO RemoteCallsAnalyzer - <operation name> calls total time: <total time, spent in remote calls>, start count: <remote calls count>, failed count: <failed remote calls count>*

**Description**

The remote and local operations duration statistics are collected for the following operations:

- Updating from local project
- Opening Manage Projects dialog
- Opening Open Server Project dialog
- Updating project
- Committing changes to a server project
- Setting selected version as latest
- Cloning project
- Managing version properties
- Opening Teamwork Cloud project
- Renaming project
- Removing project
- Adding project to server
- Viewing element history
- Comparing projects
- Using server project
- Discarding changes
- Locking and unlocking elements/diagrams
- Changing used project version

Example:

- INFO RemoteCallsAnalyzer - Manage projects all operations time 9797 ms
- INFO RemoteCallsAnalyzer - Manage projects calls total time: 2293 ms, start count: 47, failed count: 0

##### Common Features

###### Validation

The validation engine helps to keep the model accurate, complete, and correct. Since model checking is a complicated task that greatly depends on the project size, please consider the following recommendations to keep the modeling tool running smoothly:

- Set the Validation Scope to narrow down the list of validated elements.
- Set the Validate Only Visible Diagrams project option to true to limit a validation scope to visible diagrams only. Since 2021x version, all projects are migrated to validate only visible diagrams. This is a key option to keep the modeling tool running smoothly while working on large models.
- Set the Exclude Elements from Used Read-Only Project project option to true to skip the validation of elements used projects contain. [ATTACHMENT filename='validation_project_options.png']

###### Querying the Model

Ineffectively written queries can cause showstopper problems, especially when a project becomes quite large. The most attention should be paid to Script, Find, and Find Usage in Diagrams operations. Be aware that the slow execution of queries can noticeably impact the modeling experience - some operations trigger an expression (e.g., smart packages and derived properties) recalculation.

- The Find operation should not be performed for the whole project scope when specifying expressions for smart packages, derived properties, validation rules, etc. Since the operation is highly dependent on the search scope - the wider it is, the longer it takes - it can noticeably impact the modeling experience by slowing down the common modeling tool commands that, as a result, cause the expression recalculation.
- It is not a good practice to use Find for searching common elements (e.g., Class, Package, Block, etc.).
- Searching element usages in diagrams should be used carefully since the operation searches for element usages in dynamic content. UsageInDiagrams is DeprecatedThis operation is very expensive. It not only checks symbolic diagrams for elements but also loads and builds Generic Tables, Dependency Matrices, Relationship Maps, etc. It should be best avoided and used only if you understand the possible consequence of building all diagrams in the project.Instead of it, please use the **UsageInDiagramsWithSymbols**operation****that searches for usages only in symbolic diagrams. Other diagrams, such as Generic Tables, Dependency Matrices, Relationship Maps, etc., are not searched. This operation performs fast and does not load any diagrams to get the information.
- Smart Package specifics:
  - How does this affect performance? The Smart Package content is calculated on demand and cached. Next time on content request, the cached value is used. The cache is cleared on any model change. So the impact on performance depends on the performance of the content calculation, content request frequency, and model change frequency. Examples :
    - Model Browser: if a Smart Package is visible in the Model Browser, it will be refreshed each time after the cache is invalidated (model change occurs, e.g., a new element is created or an existing element is removed). So if a Smart Package is added directly under the root, it will always be visible in the Containment Tree (after opening a project, the root is expanded by default).
    - Validation: if the Smart Package is within the validation scope and the "Exclude elements from additional content" option is disabled, running validation will recalculate Smart Packages if the cache is invalidated.
    - Suspect links: Show Suspicions action triggers the calculation of Smart Packages that fall within the Tracking Scope. Starting with 2021x, the calculation of Smart Packages is no longer triggered.
    - Tables/matrices: if the Smart Package is within the table/matrix scope, it will be calculated by default. Starting with 2021x Refresh1, the content of Smart Packages is not included by default - the Include Additional Content table/matrix option was introduced for that.
  - The Use In Selection Dialogs option - set to false if a slow-running query is defined. [ATTACHMENT filename='Use_In_Selection_Dialogs_option.png']
  - Avoid nesting Smart Packages, i.e., using one Smart Package as part of another Smart Package's content.
  - Freezing the contents of a Smart Package . If you do not want the contents of the Smart Package to be updated automatically, you can suspend the calculation of the Smart Package contents. As a result, elements gathered according to a set of criteria specified in the Query dialog are no longer automatically added to the Smart Package contents. You can undo this at any time by changing the Frozen state.

The chart below shows how the Legend item condition execution time differs by performing the same expression in different languages. We advise choosing the most efficiently performing languages which are indicated as recommended (StructuredExpression, Groovy).

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']A chart comparing the Legend item condition execution time by language.

###### Troubleshooting

You can use the Evaluate Operation Performance capability to see the performance diagnostics that help determine the reasons behind slow tool operations if they are caused by the execution of expressions (e.g., derived properties, smart packages, legends, validation rules) defined in the model. Matrix and table building can also be analyzed.

For example, you noticed that opening a particular diagram took longer than expected. A progress bar titled "Adorning" popped up for a few seconds before the diagram opened. To know what caused this performance issue, run the Evaluate Operation Performance tool. In its dialog, click the 'Start' button, thenopen the diagram again reproducing the slow operation, and onceit is done, click the 'Stop' button to see the results. You can use this information to investigate the particular expression and improve its efficiency.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

More information can be found [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Evaluating operation performance' space=''].

###### Diagrams

The consumed memory increases while working with the project for some time. One of the reasons is the number of open diagrams. As high memory usage becomes a problem, we recommend that you do not keep unused diagrams open. To prevent keeping a large number of open diagrams, beginning with version 2021x, the open diagrams limit was introduced with 20 diagrams set as the default value.

Please find below how memory consumption increases depending on the size of a diagram/table:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Chart depicting memory consumption by diagram size (symbols/rows displayed in a diagram/table).

- The matrix/table building time greatly depends on the specified scope - the wider it is, the more time it takes to build the matrix/table.
- Please avoid setting the entire model as the scope even if the matrix/table building performs well, as it can cause unacceptable performance in the following circumstances:
  - a project containing such a table/matrix is used in another project;
  - the model evolves over time, so the table will eventually reach unscalable sizes.
- Also, it is not advisable to choose more than 10 element types to be displayed because this may lead to a decrease in the matrix/table building time. Since matrices/tables have an option to define a scope query, the Smart Packages should not be set as scopes as well.

###### Working with Large Tables

The process of calculating all data in large tables (containing thousands of rows) can be slow and inefficient. For the most efficient performance, we suggest the following:

- Avoid creating tables that exceed ±50 000 cells as that can affect the performance of actions performed on the table data; in such a case, it is recommended to create multiple tables instead.
- Switching to partial loading. Data is loaded only in the visible area, so the most common tasks are performed quickly. More information can be found [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Partial loading in tables' space=''] . [ATTACHMENT filename='partial_loading_options.png']

###### Working with Model Browser

The organization of the model is determined by the package hierarchy you create to partition the elements in the model into logically cohesive groups. This affects the model browser performance as well - to avoid sluggish browsing in Containment Tree, you need to organize your model to avoid 10k and more directly owned elements by the particular element. Since the containment model browser is a lazy tree, it calculates and represents the nodes while expanding the owner. A large number of owned elements can cause unexpected waiting time to complete the task. So, we recommend splitting elements by creating different packages.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A model structured into packages.

##### Virtual Environments

- Two issues have been identified in VMWare ESX environments. To help prevent problems on the Teamwork Cloud side, we have come up with recommended hardware configurations.

1. 
  1. Overcommitting of resources . The same host has an over-allocation of CPU/RAM. The bigger problem is the over-allocation of RAM. For example, let's say a machine has 1TB of physical RAM. You allocate more than 1TB across various VM's in the host. What happens is that the RAM is no longer physical RAM, but rather mapped to disk, so performance decreases. Xen (Citrix) and KVM do not allow you to over-commit. In these, you can allocate min/max but never exceed the physical RAM, and as more instances start using up the RAM, the system reduces the allocated memory.
  2. Thin disk provisioning . This arises from an over-allocation of disk resources. Let's say you have a given amount of disk space to be used among VM's. In many cases, the admin does not know how much the actual disk usage is required per machine. Some VM's may use more, some less. Therefore, you over-allocate the storage using thin provisioning. Under thin provisioning, only the amount of storage being used is allocated to the virtual disk. As utilization increases, the virtual disk (which is a file) grows. This, however, has a negative impact on the performance of input/output-intensive applications like databases. For Cassandra, it is a killer in performance because compaction can potentially temporarily double the on-disk size. All disks must be thick provisioned.

- The other item is that, at the very least, you need to be using SAS-SSD. If using SAS-SSD, the host needs to have high-end caching controllers. On the Teamwork Cloud side, the core count is important - the more cores, the better.
- SAN's are not recommended. Only NVMe (all flash storage) will provide the necessary performance for large installations, provided that they are properly configured and that enough bandwidth is allocated to the instances.
- It is recommended that you stay away from Windows deployments. There is no freely available Cassandra Windows installer.
- If we look at cloud hosting, the older instances in Amazon use the Xen hypervisor (xcp), whereas the new ones use the kvm hypervisor. These are the instances you should use: preferably, the NVMe-based i3 instances, next would be r5, and finally m5. What is critical is to provision the filesystem properly.
- On the client side, the same issues of resources over-allocation can be a performance killer. Thin provisioning affects performance because disk caches are used extensively. Therefore, what is more, important when using VDI's is working on machines that have faster processors. When provisioning the VDI's, we do not recommend that you allocate a lot of CPU's because they will not be used. It is important to have the fastest processors.

##### Antivirus

Since some antivirus software can cause a significant performance decrease upon opening a project and performing other actions, we recommend that you exclude the modeling tool's home directory from the antivirus scanning scope.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Product performance can be highly dependent on the model organization strategy. From long-term work experience with various clients, we have selected the biggest challenges and gathered their possible solutions in one place.</p><h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5e2b8224-4af5-41e7-bad9-16cf4775b4fa"><ac:parameter ac:name="">Teamwork Cloud Operations Specifics</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Teamwork Cloud Operations Specifics</h3><h4>Project Opening</h4><p>Opening Teamwork Cloud project for the first time may take a bit longer, as all project-related data has to be downloaded from the server and then processed in the modeling client. The download time depends on the project size and network speed, e.g., for large projects (containing 5M/3.5M model elements in &quot;19.0 and before/2021x and later&quot; version), it may require downloading 1GB or more of data. Once the project is opened, we store your project data locally. The processing time depends on the downloaded project size. Opening the project for the second time is much faster as there is no need to request all data from the server again. Instead, the previously downloaded and locally stored data is reused. Only new information has to be downloaded from the server. The maximum processed data count is specified by the <strong>Recent Files List Size</strong> environment option. The processed project data is always preserved when the project is saved offline. <br />More information can be found <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/CM/?contextKey=opening-projects-from-teamwork-cloud&amp;version=latest">here</a>.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="open_server_project.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Open Server Project dialog.</h6><h4>Committing</h4><p>Frequent commits make it easier for other team members to track project changes; however, this should be avoided if many users are working on a particular project. Frequent commits create a lot of project versions and increase the commit time due to the need to update the project every time. You need to decide upon the best-committing strategy within your company. A widely used practice is to commit the changes at the end of the work day; however, this should be avoided because, in some cases, the project commit may take longer than expected. <br />More information can be found <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/CM/?contextKey=committing-changes-to-teamwork-cloud&amp;version=latest">here</a>.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Committing is necessary for other team members to see the changes you've made " ac:alt="Committing is necessary for other team members to see the changes you've made "><ri:attachment ri:filename="working_scheme.png" /></ac:image></p><h4>Switching Used Project versions</h4><p>Teamwork Cloud projects are usually built from smaller parts. Different teams simultaneously develop different parts of the project, so there is a need to keep the model up-to-date. A user is notified when a new version of the used project arrives, and it is up to the user whether to apply the update or not. As not all used project version updates are important, there is no need to update the version each time a new version arrives. Therefore, it is best to set up a version update strategy within the teams. You can decide whether or not to switch to a new used project version by considering the severity of that version – the commit tags can be used to mark that the particular version contains the major changes and should be used instead. <br />More information can be found <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/CM/?contextKey=changing-used-project-versions&amp;version=latest">here</a>.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Update_used_projects.PNG" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Notification about version changes.</h6><p>In some cases, the project version update may take longer than expected due to a local cache update.</p><p><strong>Used Projects Auto Update Plugin</strong>. This plugin allows users to set up specific used projects to update. We recommend that you schedule it to launch at night so that the latest versions will be ready to use the next work day. More information can be found <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Used Projects Auto Updater Plugin" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[here]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><h4>Merge</h4><p>To perform a merge operation, the ancestor and contributor (source and target) projects have to be loaded, meaning that the memory consumption increases at least two times compared to the memory required to perform daily tasks in the project. After testing the memory consumption with different-sized projects, the following minimum memory requirement for the merge operation was identified: <em> <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">2 x (memory required to load project) + 1 GB*</span> </em> <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">.<br /></span>*These memory requirements are indicative and may vary by project. It strongly depends on the number of detected changes between merged versions (in this case, the project merge was done with 1.5k changes in total).</p><p>Since the merge operation uses a high amount of memory, please make sure that your machine is capable of performing this task. If the required memory exceeds the maximum amount of memory that can be allocated to the tool, the dedicated hardware should be used instead. In case of excessive memory usage, the merge operation can be optimized for lower memory consumption by excluding differing diagrams/elements count (<strong>Optimize for</strong> &gt; <strong>Memory</strong> in the <strong>Merge Projects</strong> dialog); however, this optimization slows down the merge operation.<br />More information can be found <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/CM/?contextKey=model-merge-in-teamwork-cloud&amp;version=latest">here</a>.</p><h4><span>Project Decomposition</span></h4><p>Model partitioning enables a more granular model management. Despite the benefits of granularity, project decomposition should be done carefully since managing used projects can become a bottleneck from a performance perspective. Please follow the criteria below to decompose a project:</p><ul><li data-uuid="a68cfae4-1ea8-47c6-aae8-1beebdf049d7"><p>20+ active users work on the same project simultaneously. As a result, the project contains many project versions, which slow down project history-related operations. Also, a higher number of users leads to a higher number of commits, resulting in longer update times and slowing down the commit operation.</p></li><li data-uuid="4b705a8f-546b-4b89-a132-aa4d233e58c0"><p>A large number of models owned by the same person indicates that the model granularity is too small or there is a need to distribute responsibilities.</p></li><li data-uuid="7e0eb908-a836-4387-9105-0addfd32d240">The part of the project (such as type libraries) should be used in other projects.</li><li data-uuid="b117330a-56d5-4db4-8551-d41e1e210356">Different teams work on a certain aspect of the system separately. All these separate parts are then connected in the integration project.</li></ul><h4>Publishing projects to Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud</h4><p>As publishing greatly depends on the element count and diagram complexity (e.g., diagram type, the number of elements displayed, dynamic or static content, etc.), please consider the publishing scope before publishing. Since publishing uses a high amount of memory, please make sure that your machine is capable of performing this task. Remember that if the required memory exceeds the maximum amount of memory that can be allocated to the tool, the dedicated hardware should be used instead. <br />More information can be found <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/MCS/?contextKey=publishing-documents&amp;version=latest&amp;variant=Teamwork%20Cloud%20and%20Services">here</a>.</p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="main_workflow_publishing.png" /></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Publishing a model is the first step in the workflow for reviewing models and providing feedback.</h6><h4>Content History</h4><p style="margin-left: 0.0in;text-align: justify;">If retrieving a particular element history takes a lot of time, it implies that the Content History action should be performed carefully as it retrieves the history of all contained elements at once. Before invoking this action, you should carefully consider the scope of the elements contained by the element for which the action is invoked. <br />More information can be found <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/CM/?contextKey=reviewing-content-history&amp;version=latest">here</a>.<ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Content_history.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">History of element changes in the scope of a <em>Book </em>package.</h6><h3>Teamwork Cloud Operations Troubleshooting</h3><p><strong>Waiting for Response</strong></p><p>If the operation that requires a remote call to Teamwork Cloud is taking too long, you can analyze a <em>.log</em> file to learn how much time was spent processing the operation on the client and server-side.</p><p class="title">Where do I find a .<em>log</em> file?</p><ol><li data-uuid="eb64db85-0693-4c39-a208-4d649cfa3cde"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Open your modeling tool.</span></li><li data-uuid="92a15eba-2849-4e22-9484-f827b214a4e5"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the main menu, go to </span><strong>Help</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> &gt;</span> <strong>About &lt;modeling tool name&gt;</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">.</span></li><li data-uuid="7b61310f-20ce-476b-8653-c254c0267fca"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the </span><strong>Environment </strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">tab, click the file path next to</span> <strong>Log File </strong> <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">to open a <em>.log</em> file.</span></li></ol><p><strong>Measuring Network Latency</strong></p><p><em>INFO  NetworkPerformance - &lt;operation name&gt; latency &lt;latency time&gt; </em></p><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>The latency (i.e., the time it takes for the ping signal sent from the tool to the server to return) is measured for the following operations:</p><ul><li data-uuid="83e58b0c-ab93-4087-9778-69fa33c697af">Login</li><li data-uuid="ac13a91b-8e0e-4c90-aab6-4ac042bbda75">Committing project to server</li><li data-uuid="cfbefb68-fbc1-44d8-965d-ccccafea3edd">Updating project</li><li data-uuid="029d6b99-6b2d-406f-a762-8de9d27afc17">Opening project<br /><br /></li></ul><p class="title">Example: INFO  NetworkPerformance - Login latency 1 ms</p><p><strong>Remote Operations Duration Statistics</strong></p><p><em>INFO  RemoteCallsAnalyzer - &lt;operation name&gt; all operations time &lt;total operation time&gt;</em></p><p><em>INFO  RemoteCallsAnalyzer - &lt;operation name&gt; calls total time: &lt;total time, spent in remote calls&gt;, start count: &lt;remote calls count&gt;, failed count: &lt;failed remote calls count&gt;</em></p><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>The remote and local operations duration statistics are collected for the following operations:</p><ul><li data-uuid="7192d014-9d1d-4b4c-a43b-4d98737a8fb1">Updating from local project</li><li data-uuid="f9c4a982-06c6-469b-9e55-6f0d7bc8da69">Opening<strong> Manage Projects</strong> dialog</li><li data-uuid="e01bad69-4140-4398-b004-0430f080a8d7">Opening <strong>Open Server Project</strong> dialog</li><li data-uuid="1f5c7d7d-26ea-4726-9abc-2671a0f488e0">Updating project</li><li data-uuid="b6571f29-ba65-45fd-820b-bbf5f649ce44">Committing changes to a server project</li><li data-uuid="6b2df5c4-d600-48c1-9170-413e7885450b">Setting selected version as latest</li><li data-uuid="a6350a99-4870-47c6-825a-39dec645eca7">Cloning project</li><li data-uuid="97cd3a33-45fd-47b9-ae08-cfce3f4531c8">Managing version properties</li><li data-uuid="7a0750be-bd29-4d3b-b748-72644f045a5b">Opening Teamwork Cloud project</li><li data-uuid="752714ae-ad38-4e45-a795-817c5fd626a1">Renaming project</li><li data-uuid="1dc6af7a-8950-47e4-b057-d5755fddfe67">Removing project</li><li data-uuid="adf6cdd5-b139-482b-9105-c4583adc3fb3">Adding project to server</li><li data-uuid="75775711-d887-4748-a160-ca7e67eca10b">Viewing element history</li><li data-uuid="86a99280-30d2-46ac-a901-c5f8b168efed">Comparing projects</li><li data-uuid="5b58d4b1-b8d2-4ac9-a06e-ad86828ab56b">Using server project</li><li data-uuid="977966c9-61cb-4027-bc72-86a6fd1a3373">Discarding changes</li><li data-uuid="bf64b80b-536f-438b-9e24-1a21c12a9b40">Locking and unlocking elements/diagrams</li><li data-uuid="e44c8740-72ea-477e-b985-bf3b7cd845d0">Changing used project version</li></ul><p class="title">Example: </p><ul><li data-uuid="4884fb67-7cc1-420a-8ae6-63551a779a0f">INFO  RemoteCallsAnalyzer - Manage projects all operations time 9797 ms</li><li data-uuid="5356167d-111b-4780-abe6-b54410899ff3">INFO  RemoteCallsAnalyzer - Manage projects calls total time: 2293 ms, start count: 47, failed count: 0</li></ul><h3>Common Features</h3><h4>Validation</h4><p>The validation engine helps to keep the model accurate, complete, and correct. Since model checking is a complicated task that greatly depends on the project size, please consider the following recommendations to keep the modeling tool running smoothly:</p><ul><li data-uuid="1c504bb6-7dc9-4864-bd48-cddfe1a35581">Set the <strong>Validation Scope</strong><em> </em>to narrow down the list of validated elements.</li><li data-uuid="27f1334d-0fd3-4348-b9db-4d7daea8916b">Set the <strong>Validate Only Visible Diagrams</strong><em> </em>project option to <em>true </em>to limit a validation scope to visible diagrams only. This operation performs fast and does not load any diagrams to get the information.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li data-uuid="109ba95e-712c-4649-8ba5-00f542ecfccc">Smart Package specifics:<br /><ul><li><strong>How does this affect performance? </strong>The Smart Package content is calculated on demand and cached. Next time on content request, the cached value is used. The cache is cleared on any model change. So the impact on performance depends on the performance of the content calculation, content request frequency, and model change frequency.<br /><strong>Examples</strong>:<ul><li>Model Browser: if a Smart Package is visible in the Model Browser, it will be refreshed each time after the cache is invalidated (model change occurs, e.g., a new element is created or an existing element is removed). Starting with 2021x, the calculation of Smart Packages is no longer triggered.</li><li>Tables/matrices: if the Smart Package is within the table/matrix scope, it will be calculated by default. Starting with 2021x Refresh1, the content of Smart Packages is not included by default - the <strong>Include Additional Content</strong> table/matrix option was introduced for that.</li></ul></li><li>The <strong>Use In Selection Dialogs</strong> option - set to <em>false</em> if a slow-running query is defined.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Use_In_Selection_Dialogs_option.png" /></ac:image></li><li>Avoid nesting Smart Packages, i.e., using one Smart Package as part of another Smart Package's content.</li><li><strong>Freezing the contents of a Smart Package</strong>. If you do not want the contents of the Smart Package to be updated automatically, you can suspend the calculation of the Smart Package contents. As a result, elements gathered according to a set of criteria specified in the <strong>Query</strong> dialog are no longer automatically added to the Smart Package contents. You can undo this at any time by changing the <strong>Frozen</strong> state. <br /><ac:image ac:alt="frozen_enabled.png"><ri:url ri:value="https://docs.nomagic.com/download/attachments/70410160/frozen_enabled.png?version=1&amp;modificationDate=1616502291005&amp;api=v2" /></ac:image></li></ul></li></ul><p>The chart below shows how the Legend item condition execution time differs by performing the same expression in different languages. We advise choosing the most efficiently performing languages which are indicated as recommended (StructuredExpression, Groovy).</p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Scripting_languages_performance.png" /></ac:image><span style="color:var(--ds-background-accent-gray-bolder,#626f86);">A chart comparing the Legend item condition execution time by language.</span></h6><h4>Troubleshooting</h4><p>You can use the Evaluate Operation Performance capability to see the performance diagnostics that help determine the reasons behind slow tool operations if they are caused by the execution of expressions (e.g., derived properties, smart packages, legends, validation rules) defined in the model. Matrix and table building can also be analyzed.</p><p><span>For example, you noticed that opening a particular diagram took longer than expected. A progress bar titled &quot;Adorning&quot; popped up for a few seconds before the diagram opened. To know what caused this performance issue, run the Evaluate Operation Performance tool. In its dialog, click </span> <span>the 'Start' button, then </span><span>open the diagram again reproducing the slow operation, and once </span><span>it is done, click the 'Stop' button to see the results. </span> <span>You can use this information to investigate the particular expression and improve its efficiency.</span></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Evaluating_operation_performance.png" /></ac:image></p><p class="auto-cursor-target">More information can be found <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Evaluating operation performance" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[here]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><h4>Diagrams</h4><p style="margin-left: 0.0cm;">The consumed memory increases while working with the project for some time. One of the reasons is the number of open diagrams. As high memory usage becomes a problem, we recommend that you do not keep unused diagrams open. To prevent keeping a large number of open diagrams, beginning with version 2021x, the open diagrams limit was introduced with 20 diagrams set as the default value.</p><p style="margin-left: 0.0cm;">Please find below how memory consumption increases depending on the size of a diagram/table:</p><p style="margin-left: 0.0cm;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="memory_consumption_of_tables_and_diagrams_with_symbols.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 0.0cm;">Chart depicting memory consumption by diagram size (symbols/rows displayed in a diagram/table).</h6><ul><li data-uuid="8ee859c5-0c59-4662-bb9a-03bf29a44ca6">The matrix/table building time greatly depends on the specified scope - the wider it is, the more time it takes to build the matrix/table. </li><li style="list-style-type: none;" data-uuid="bd2b5de4-bc9e-4937-a75e-870c94cfcdcd"><p>Please avoid setting the entire model as the scope even if the matrix/table building performs well, as it can cause unacceptable performance in the following circumstances:</p><ul><li>a project containing such a table/matrix is used in another project;</li><li>the model evolves over time, so the table will eventually reach unscalable sizes.</li></ul></li><li data-uuid="a32aecab-07ed-4c3e-bbef-1eacb15942ba">Also, it is not advisable to choose more than 10 element types to be displayed because this may lead to a decrease in the matrix/table building time. Since matrices/tables have an option to define a scope query, the Smart Packages should not be set as scopes as well.</li></ul><h4>Working with Large Tables</h4><p>The process of calculating all data in large tables (containing thousands of rows) can be slow and inefficient. For the most efficient performance, we suggest the following:</p><ul><li data-uuid="4e4f0fa0-1233-44e0-b092-04162d627399">Avoid creating tables that exceed ±50 000 cells as that can affect the performance of actions performed on the table data; in such a case, it is recommended to create multiple tables instead.</li><li data-uuid="aa31587f-c171-491c-9577-9ae82d3d56f8">Switching to partial loading. Data is loaded only in the visible area, so the most common tasks are performed quickly. More information can be found <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Partial loading in tables" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[here]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="partial_loading_options.png" /></ac:image></li></ul><h4>Working with Model Browser</h4><p class="p1">The organization of the model is determined by the package hierarchy you create to partition the elements in the model into logically cohesive groups. This affects the model browser performance as well - to avoid sluggish browsing in Containment Tree, you need to organize your model to avoid 10k and more directly owned elements by the particular element. Since the containment model browser is a lazy tree, it calculates and represents the nodes while expanding the owner. A large number of owned elements can cause unexpected waiting time to complete the task. So, we recommend splitting elements by creating different packages.</p><p class="p1"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="model_browser_structure.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 0.0cm;">A model structured into packages.</h6><h3>Virtual Environments</h3><ul><li data-uuid="738ecb6b-e0c8-4251-913e-0b50ca485c6b">Two issues have been identified in VMWare ESX environments. To help prevent problems on the Teamwork Cloud side, we have come up with recommended hardware configurations.</li></ul><ol><li style="list-style-type: none;background-image: none;" data-uuid="86b678f6-f9ea-443f-b204-a27f37d1d529"><ol><li><strong>Overcommitting of resources</strong>. The same host has an over-allocation of CPU/RAM. The bigger problem is the over-allocation of RAM. For example, let's say a machine has 1TB of physical RAM. You allocate more than 1TB across various VM's in the host. What happens is that the RAM is no longer physical RAM, but rather mapped to disk, so performance decreases. Xen (Citrix) and KVM do not allow you to over-commit. In these, you can allocate min/max but never exceed the physical RAM, and as more instances start using up the RAM, the system reduces the allocated memory.</li><li><strong>Thin disk provisioning</strong>. This arises from an over-allocation of disk resources. Let's say you have a given amount of disk space to be used among VM's. In many cases, the admin does not know how much the actual disk usage is required per machine. Some VM's may use more, some less. Therefore, you over-allocate the storage using thin provisioning. Under thin provisioning, only the amount of storage being used is allocated to the virtual disk. As utilization increases, the virtual disk (which is a file) grows. This, however, has a negative impact on the performance of input/output-intensive applications like databases. For Cassandra, it is a killer in performance because compaction can potentially temporarily double the on-disk size. All disks must be thick provisioned.</li></ol></li></ol><ul><li data-uuid="d8ab37f7-5290-4746-882c-142a101e42eb">The other item is that, at the very least, you need to be using SAS-SSD. If using SAS-SSD, the host needs to have high-end caching controllers. On the Teamwork Cloud side, the core count is important - the more cores, the better.</li><li data-uuid="e462b98c-13af-49d7-8441-5f2ec537197f">SAN's are not recommended. Only NVMe (all flash storage) will provide the necessary performance for large installations, provided that they are properly configured and that enough bandwidth is allocated to the instances.</li><li data-uuid="ffba01a4-0ca6-48ba-b577-05fac4457fa0">It is recommended that you stay away from Windows deployments. There is no freely available Cassandra Windows installer.</li><li data-uuid="4437ea2e-3461-4967-9b8d-5f4e77b696ff">If we look at cloud hosting, the older instances in Amazon use the Xen hypervisor (xcp), whereas the new ones use the kvm hypervisor. These are the instances you should use: preferably, the NVMe-based i3 instances, next would be r5, and finally m5. What is critical is to provision the filesystem properly.</li><li data-uuid="cd8f661d-f28d-475f-8261-9102284b6e8a">On the client side, the same issues of resources over-allocation can be a performance killer. Thin provisioning affects performance because disk caches are used extensively. Therefore, what is more, important when using VDI's is working on machines that have faster processors. When provisioning the VDI's, we do not recommend that you allocate a lot of CPU's because they will not be used. It is important to have the fastest processors.</li></ul><h3>Antivirus</h3><p>Since some antivirus software can cause a significant performance decrease upon opening a project and performing other actions, we recommend that you exclude the modeling tool's home directory from the antivirus scanning scope.</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249561525 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05451: Handling inconsistencies between structure and behavior models

- page_id: `249561525`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249561525/Handling+inconsistencies+between+structure+and+behavior+models
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T17:13:06.683+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > SysML v1 modeling > Behavior to structure synchronization
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Tip

464453088

INLINE

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Tip

464453099

INLINE

464453086

INLINE

**On this page**

**3**

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Tip

464453089

INLINE

Three validation rules complement each other to check whether the structure model is compatible with the behavior model. If any inconsistencies are found, the structure model can be automatically synchronized with the behavior model using solvers. First, you need to synchronize interfaces so that you can handle Connectors and, finally, Item Flows. As a consequence, the manual work is reduced.

#### TIP: Tip

Tip

You can handle detected inconsistencies using solvers directly in:

- Internal Block Diagram
- Validation Results panel

##### Handling missing Proxy Ports and Interface Blocks

The first validation rule checks whether Proxy Ports and Interface Blocks are compatible with flows that flow between the two different Part Properties in Activities. The missing Proxy Ports and Interface Blocks can be handled automatically using solvers.

To create missing Proxy Ports and Interface Blocks

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the Internal Block Diagram, select a Part Property highlighted in yellow, and then in the smart manipulator toolbar, click [ATTACHMENT filename='triangle_symbol_smart_manipulator_toolbar.png'] . [IMAGE alt='' src='']
  - In the open **Validation Results** panel, right-click a validation result. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Select one of the following:

1. 
  - Create Port - creates a new Proxy Port typed by a compatible Interface Block.
  - - changes an already existing Proxy Port type to a compatible Interface Block.
  - Add Missing Flow Property - adds a new Flow Property to an Interface Block that types a Proxy Port.
  - Reverse Direction of Port - changes the Proxy Port direction, thus changing the Flow Property direction of an Interface Block that types a Proxy Port.

A Proxy Port typed by a compatible Interface Block is created.

###### Choosing a compatible Interface Block

If you select the **Choose Compatible Interface Block** solver, the **Choose Proxy Port with Compatible Interface Block**dialog opens.

You can now choose a Proxy Port and type it with an Interface Block that has Flow Properties compatible with the type and direction of the missing flow.

Alternatively, you can create a new Proxy Port and an Interface Block to type the Proxy Port. For this, select **<NEW>**.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Choosing a Proxy Port and specifying a compatible Interface Block as its type.

##### Handling missing Connectors

The second validation rule checks whether there is a Connector between the two compatible Proxy Ports owned by the Part Property. The missing Connectors can be handled automatically using solvers.

To create missing Connectors

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the Internal Block Diagram, select a Proxy Port highlighted in yellow, and then in the smart manipulator toolbar, click [ATTACHMENT filename='triangle_symbol_smart_manipulator_toolbar.png'] . [ATTACHMENT filename='btos_second_validation_rule.png']
  - In the open Validation Results panel, right-click a validation result. [ATTACHMENT filename='second_validation_rule_creating_connector_from_validation_results_panel.png']
2. Select Create Connector .

A Connector between the two compatible Proxy Ports is created.

##### Handling missing Item Flows

The third validation rule checks realized Item Flows in Activities and helps to ensure the correct Item Flows are realized on Connectors that connect Proxy Ports typed by compatible Interface Blocks. The missing Item Flows can be handled automatically using solvers.

To realize missing Item Flows on Connectors

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the Internal Block Diagram, select a Connector highlighted in yellow, and then in the smart manipulator toolbar, click [ATTACHMENT filename='triangle_symbol_smart_manipulator_toolbar.png'] . [ATTACHMENT filename='third_btos_validation_rule_IBD.png']
  - In the open Validation Results panel, right-click a validation result. [ATTACHMENT filename='third_btos_validation_rule_validation_results_panel.png']
2. Select one of the following:
  - Realize Missing Item Flow - realizes the compatible existing Item Flow on the Connector that was found in the behavior model.
  - Open Item Flow Manager - opens the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Item Flow Manager dialog' space=''] dialog.
  - Change Item Flow Realization Compatibility Checking - opens the Project Options dialog to enable/disable the Item Flow realization checking.

A compatible Item Flow is realized on the selected Connector.

##### Navigating to elements

To learn more about the validated elements, in the smart manipulator toolbar of the Internal Block Diagram or Validation Results panel, select the **Navigate To** command to navigate to:

- Activity Edges in the Activity diagram from which the missing flow has been identified.
- Part Properties in the Internal Block Diagram between which items are flowing.

Elements are automatically selected on the diagram pane and in the Containment tree.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Navigating to an element from the smart manipulator toolbar.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="960074de-fd04-4de9-bb65-6fffaaf7e10e"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">464453088</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="06d83478-7b74-4a66-907e-56899d0f26b6"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">464453099</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="628463d5-cf73-48eb-9fa5-9bec2d0c87a5"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">464453086</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ad388396-2752-401a-824f-40a2fa1b96e8"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">3</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro><br /></strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="44f990f7-c522-456b-b4a8-a9b098afcd74"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">464453089</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Three validation rules complement each other to check whether the structure model is compatible with the behavior model. As a consequence, the manual work is reduced.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="89c06198-dcad-4159-ab80-e997c0d437b1"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Tip</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can handle detected inconsistencies using solvers directly in:</p><ul><li>Internal Block Diagram</li><li><strong>Validation Results</strong> panel</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Handling missing Proxy Ports and Interface Blocks</h3><p>The first validation rule checks whether Proxy Ports and Interface Blocks are compatible with flows that flow between the two different Part Properties in Activities. The missing Proxy Ports and Interface Blocks can be handled automatically using solvers. </p><p>To create missing Proxy Ports and Interface Blocks</p><hr /><ol><li>Do one of the following:<ul><li>In the Internal Block Diagram, select a Part Property highlighted in yellow, and then in the smart manipulator toolbar, click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="triangle_symbol_smart_manipulator_toolbar.png" /></ac:image><span>.</span><br /><span><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="behavior_to_structure_solvers_on_IBD.png" /></ac:image><br /></span></li><li><span>In the open <strong>Validation Results</strong> panel, right-click a validation result. </span><br /><span><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="btos_validation_results_panel.png" /></ac:image><br /></span></li></ul></li><li>Select one of the following:</li></ol><ol><li style="list-style-type: none;background-image: none;"><ul><li><strong>Create Port </strong>- creates a new Proxy Port typed by a compatible Interface Block.</li><li><strong><ac:link ac:anchor="Choosing a compatible Interface Block"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Choose Compatible Interface Block]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> </strong>- changes an already existing Proxy Port type to a compatible Interface Block.<strong><br /></strong></li><li><strong>Add Missing Flow Property </strong>- adds a new Flow Property to an Interface Block that types a Proxy Port.</li><li><strong>Reverse Direction of Port </strong>- changes the Proxy Port direction, thus changing the Flow Property direction of an Interface Block that types a Proxy Port.</li></ul></li></ol><p>A Proxy Port typed by a compatible Interface Block is created. </p><h4>Choosing a compatible Interface Block</h4><p>If you select the <strong>Choose Compatible Interface Block</strong> solver, the <strong>Choose Proxy Port with Compatible Interface Block </strong>dialog opens.</p><p>You can now choose a Proxy Port and type it with an Interface Block that has Flow Properties compatible with the type and direction of the missing flow.</p><p>Alternatively, you can create a new Proxy Port and an Interface Block to type the Proxy Port. For this, select <strong>&lt;NEW&gt;</strong>.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="choosing_proxy_port_with_compatible_interface_block.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Choosing a Proxy Port and specifying a compatible Interface Block as its type.</h6><h3>Handling missing Connectors</h3><p>The second validation rule checks whether there is a Connector between the two compatible Proxy Ports owned by the Part Property. The missing Connectors can be handled automatically using solvers.</p><p>To create missing Connectors</p><hr /><ol><li>Do one of the following:<ul><li>In the Internal Block Diagram, select a Proxy Port highlighted in yellow, and then in the smart manipulator toolbar, click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="triangle_symbol_smart_manipulator_toolbar.png" /></ac:image>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="btos_second_validation_rule.png" /></ac:image></li><li>In the open <strong>Validation Results</strong> panel, right-click a validation result. <br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="second_validation_rule_creating_connector_from_validation_results_panel.png" /></ac:image></li></ul></li><li>Select <strong>Create Connector</strong>.</li></ol><p>A Connector between the two compatible Proxy Ports is created. </p><h3>Handling missing Item Flows</h3><p>The third validation rule checks realized Item Flows in Activities and helps to ensure the correct Item Flows are realized on Connectors that connect Proxy Ports typed by compatible Interface Blocks. <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="72d79904-596f-45f5-a489-96b50cce33de">The</ac:inline-comment-marker> missing Item Flows can be handled automatically using solvers.</p><p>To realize missing Item Flows on Connectors</p><hr /><ol><li>Do one of the following:<ul><li>In the Internal Block Diagram, select a Connector highlighted in yellow, and then in the smart manipulator toolbar, click<ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="triangle_symbol_smart_manipulator_toolbar.png" /></ac:image>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="third_btos_validation_rule_IBD.png" /></ac:image></li><li>In the open <strong>Validation Results</strong> panel, right-click a validation result. <br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="third_btos_validation_rule_validation_results_panel.png" /></ac:image></li></ul></li><li>Select one of the following:<ul><li><strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="57125055-8fc2-4cba-b681-24a478761656">Realize Missing Item Flow</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="57125055-8fc2-4cba-b681-24a478761656"> - </ac:inline-comment-marker><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="57125055-8fc2-4cba-b681-24a478761656">realizes the compatible existing Item Flow on the Connector that was found in the behavior model.</ac:inline-comment-marker></li><li><strong>Open Item Flow Manager</strong> - opens the <strong><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Item Flow Manager dialog" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Item Flow Manager]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></strong> dialog.</li><li><strong>Change Item Flow Realization Compatibility Checking</strong> - opens the <strong>Project Options</strong> dialog to enable/disable the Item Flow realization checking. </li></ul></li></ol><p>A compatible Item Flow is realized on the selected Connector. </p><h3>Navigating to elements</h3><p>To learn more about the validated elements, in the smart manipulator toolbar of the Internal Block Diagram or Validation Results panel, select the <strong>Navigate To</strong> command to navigate to:</p><ul><li>Activity Edges in the Activity diagram from which the missing flow has been identified.</li><li>Part Properties in the Internal Block Diagram between which items are flowing.</li></ul><p>Elements are automatically selected on the diagram pane and in the Containment tree. </p><p> <ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="navigating_to_element_IBD.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Navigating to an element from the smart manipulator toolbar.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249565344 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05452: Handling inconsistencies between structure and behavior models in UAF

- page_id: `249565344`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249565344/Handling+inconsistencies+between+structure+and+behavior+models+in+UAF
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T17:07:45.347+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > Procedures > UAF Behavior to structure synchronization
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Tip

464453088

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Tip

464453099

464453086

**On this page**

**3**

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Tip

464453089

Three validation rules complement each other to check whether the structure model is compatible with the behavior model. If any inconsistencies are found, the structure model can be automatically synchronized with the behavior model using solvers. First, you must synchronize interfaces to handle Connectors and, finally, Exchanges. As a consequence, manual work is reduced.

#### TIP: Tip

Tip

You can handle detected inconsistencies using solvers directly in:

- Internal Connectivity Diagram
- Validation Results panel

##### Handling missing Ports and Interfaces

The first validation rule checks whether Ports and Interfaces are compatible with flows between the Asset Roles. The missing Ports and Interfaces can be handled automatically using solvers.

To create missing Ports and Interfaces

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the Internal Connectivity Diagram, select an Asset Role highlighted in yellow, and then in the smart manipulator toolbar, click [ATTACHMENT filename='triangle_symbol_smart_manipulator_toolbar.png'] . [IMAGE alt='' src='']
  - In the open **Validation Results** panel, right-click a validation result. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. Select one of the following:

1. 
  - Create <viewpoint name> Port - creates a new Port typed by a compatible Interface.
  - Interface]]> - changes an existing Port type to a compatible Interface.
  - Add Missing Flow Property - adds a new Flow Property to an Interface that types a Port.
  - Reverse Direction of <viewpoint name> Port - changes the Port direction, thus changing the Flow Property direction of an Interface that types a Port.

A Port typed by a compatible Interface is created.

###### Choosing a compatible Interface

If you select the **Choose Compatible Interface**solver, the **Choose Port with Compatible Interface**dialog opens.

You can now choose a Port and type it with an Interface with Flow Properties compatible with the type and direction of the missing flow.

Alternatively, you can create a new Port and an Interface to type the Port. For this, select **<NEW>**.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Choosing a Port and specifying a compatible Interface as its type.

##### Handling missing Connectors

The second validation rule checks whether a Connector exists between the two compatible Ports owned by the Asset Role. The missing Connectors can be handled automatically using solvers.

To create missing Connectors

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the Internal Connectivity Diagram, select a Port highlighted in yellow, and then in the smart manipulator toolbar, click [ATTACHMENT filename='triangle_symbol_smart_manipulator_toolbar.png'] . [ATTACHMENT filename='UAF_behavior_to_structure_connectors_validation_rule.png']
  - In the open Validation Results panel, right-click a validation result. [ATTACHMENT filename='UAF_behavior_to_structure_connectors_validation_rule_panel.png']
2. Select Create <viewpoint name> Connector .

A Connector between the two compatible Ports is created.

##### Handling missing Exchanges

The third validation rule checks realized Exchanges in Activities and help to ensure the correct Exchanges are realized on Connectors that connect Ports typed by compatible Interface. The missing Exchanges can be handled automatically using solvers.

To realize missing Exchanges on Connectors

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the Internal Connectivity Diagram, select a Connector highlighted in yellow, and then in the smart manipulator toolbar, click [ATTACHMENT filename='triangle_symbol_smart_manipulator_toolbar.png'] . [ATTACHMENT filename='UAF_behavior_to_structure_exchanges_validation_rule.png']
  - In the open Validation Results panel, right-click a validation result. [ATTACHMENT filename='UAF_behavior_to_structure_exchanges_validation_rule_panel.png']
2. Select one of the following:
  - Realize Missing <viewpoint name> Exchange - realizes existing compatible Exchange found in the behavior model on the Connector.
  - Open <viewpoint name> Exchange Manager - opens the Exchange Manager* dialog .
  - Change Exchange Realization Compatibility Checking - opens the Project Options dialog to enable/disable the Exchange realization checking.

A compatible Exchange is realized on the selected Connector.

##### Navigating to elements

To learn more about the validated elements, in the smart manipulator toolbar of the Internal Connectivity Diagram or Validation Results panel, select the **Navigate To** command to navigate to:

- Activity Edges in the Process Flow diagram from which the missing flow has been identified.
- Asset Roles in the Internal Connectivity Diagram between which items are flowing.

Elements are automatically selected on the diagram pane and in the Containment tree.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Navigating to an element from the smart manipulator toolbar.

1851350132

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Exchange Manager dialog' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Service Exchange Manager dialog' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Resource Exchange Manager dialog' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="960074de-fd04-4de9-bb65-6fffaaf7e10e"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">464453088</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="06d83478-7b74-4a66-907e-56899d0f26b6"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">464453099</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="628463d5-cf73-48eb-9fa5-9bec2d0c87a5"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">464453086</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ad388396-2752-401a-824f-40a2fa1b96e8"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">3</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro><br /></strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="731914e9-b302-4ea9-98ea-810d7a18d03a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">464453089</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Three validation rules complement each other to check whether the structure model is compatible with the behavior model. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243968414 space=MT version=1 -->
````
## PAGE 05454: Hide Uncommon Elements filter

- page_id: `243968414`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243968414/Hide+Uncommon+Elements+filter
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:50:58.630+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Working with model elements > Selecting elements
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1827084503

1827084505

1827084504

You can enable the *Hide Uncommon Elements* filter in the *Select Type* dialog to leave out uncommon element types and thus find the ones you need easier.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Hide Uncommon Elements filter in the Select Type dialog

Once *Hide Uncommon Elements*is enabled, the**filter returns only those elements whose types are based on:

- Class (except Metaclass and Term)
- Component
- Signal
- Interface
- DataType
- Enumeration
- PrimitiveType
- AssociationClass

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Specified property type

If the SysML profile is loaded, only elements of*Block*and*ValueType*types are listed in the *Select Type* dialog.

If the Stereotype tag types are being specified, metaclasses are not filtered out.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Metaclasses in the *Select Type* dialog

1827084502

**Related Pages:**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Selecting port and part types' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249561265 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05455: Hiding direction arrow on port shape

- page_id: `249561265`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249561265/Hiding+direction+arrow+on+port+shape
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T17:00:19.025+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > SysML v1 modeling > Modeling structure with Blocks > Defining interfaces using ports > Creating ports
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To hide the direction arrow on [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Proxy Port' space='MED'] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Flow Port' space='MED'] shape

1. Right-click the Proxy Port or Flow Port shape and select the Symbol Properties .
2. In the Symbol Properties dialog, set the Show Port Direction property value to false.
3. Click Close . The direction arrow is hidden on the selected port shape.

The direction arrow of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Full Port' space='MED'] shape is hidden by default. To display it, set the **Show Port Direction** property value to *true* in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Symbol Properties dialog' space='']**Symbol Properties** dialog.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>To hide the direction arrow on <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Proxy Port" /></ac:link> or <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Flow Port" /></ac:link> shape</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the Proxy Port or Flow Port shape and select the <strong>Symbol Properties</strong>.</li><li>In the <strong>Symbol Properties</strong> dialog, set the <strong>Show Port Direction</strong> property value to <em>false.</em></li><li>Click <strong>Close</strong>.<br />The direction arrow is hidden on the selected port shape.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a8be4db4-b70c-46f9-b22d-14251cd8a000"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The direction arrow of the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Full Port" /></ac:link> shape is hidden by default. To display it, set the <strong>Show Port Direction</strong> property value to <em>true</em> in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Symbol Properties dialog" /><ac:link-body><strong>Symbol Properties</strong> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249561267 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05456: Hiding the multiplicity

- page_id: `249561267`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249561267/Hiding+the+multiplicity
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T17:00:19.452+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > SysML v1 modeling > Modeling structure with Blocks > Defining interfaces using ports > Creating ports
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can hide the multiplicity next to the port andPart Property type.

To hide the multiplicity

1. Right-click the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Flow Port' space='MED'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Full Port' space='MED'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Proxy Port' space='MED'], or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Part Property' space='MED'] shape and select**Symbol Properties**.
2. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Symbol Properties dialog' space='']**Symbol Properties** dialog, set the **Show Multiplicity** property value to *false*.
3. Click **Close**.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">You can hide the multiplicity next to the <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">port and </span><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Part Property type</span>.</span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">To hide the multiplicity <br /></span></p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Right-click the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Flow Port" /></ac:link>, <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Full Port" /></ac:link></span>, <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Proxy Port" /></ac:link></span>, or <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Part Property" /></ac:link></span> shape and select<strong> Symbol Properties</strong>.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">In the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Symbol Properties dialog" /><ac:link-body><strong>Symbol Properties</strong> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link>, set the <strong>Show Multiplicity</strong> property value to <em>false</em>.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Click <strong>Close</strong>.</span></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243969646 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05457: Hierarchy Diagram Wizard

- page_id: `243969646`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243969646/Hierarchy+Diagram+Wizard
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:51:34.548+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Model analysis > Model Visualizer
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This functionality is available in Standard, Professional, Architect, and Enterprise editions only.

The **Hierarchy Diagram Wizard** collects the largest hierarchies and allows each of them to be displayed either as separate diagrams or all in one diagram.

To create a diagram using the **Hierarchy Diagram Wizard**

1. Open the Hierarchy Diagram Wizard by doing one of the following:
  - On the Diagrams menu, select Diagram Wizards > Hierarchy Diagram Wizard .
  - From the Analyze menu, select Model Visualizer . In the open Model Visualizer dialog, select to open the Hierarchy Diagram Wizard and click **Start** .
2. Follow the steps as directed by the wizard.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="33df9e1d-7457-4043-ac81-6afd9c3c69da"><ac:rich-text-body><p>This functionality is available in Standard, Professional, Architect, and Enterprise editions only.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The <strong>Hierarchy Diagram Wizard</strong> collects the largest hierarchies and allows each of them to be displayed either as separate diagrams or all in one diagram.</p><p> </p><p>To create a diagram using the <strong>Hierarchy Diagram Wizard</strong></p><hr /><ol><li>Open the <strong>Hierarchy Diagram Wizard</strong><strong> </strong>by doing one of the following:<br /><ul><li>On the <strong>Diagrams</strong> menu, select <strong>Diagram Wizards</strong> &gt; <strong>Hierarchy Diagram Wizard</strong>.</li><li>From the <strong>Analyze</strong> menu, select <strong>Model Visualizer</strong>. In the open Model Visualizer dialog, select to open the <strong>Hierarchy Diagram Wizard</strong> and click <strong><strong>Start</strong></strong>.</li></ul></li><li>Follow the steps as directed by the wizard.</li></ol>
````
