In the Model Browser, you will see the <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="hyperlinkElementAdornment.gif" /></ac:image> Hyperlinks/Go To icon on the element. On the diagram pane, on the symbol, you will see the icon representing the subject the hyperlink is added to. You can instantly open a hyperlink by double clicking an element or diagram.</p></li></ol></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="db317641-094c-4871-a9bc-9b2c57eb49db"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Defining hyperlinks" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><ul><li data-uuid="cd33e016-4e7e-47a9-a337-89dc3c335978"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/CDH/?contextKey=linking-model-elements-to-oslc-resources&amp;version=latest">Linking model elements to OSLC resources</a></li><li data-uuid="9d40a51e-c0bf-4a86-8c5f-a1ecfec0bf86"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/DEVG/?contextKey=managing-hyperlinks&amp;version=latest">Managing hyperlinks</a></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243969394 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05474: Identifying Package Dependencies

- page_id: `243969394`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243969394/Identifying+Package+Dependencies
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:51:27.601+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Model analysis
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In a model, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Package' space='MED'] can be associated with other packages or model elements in a project scope or outside the project scope. In other words, packages can have relations with external elements in used projects, shared libraries, profiles, etc. An element depends on the used project when at least one of its metaproperties reference elements from at least one share of that used project. In this case, the element depends on that used project.

To avoid any violations on editing a package, especially on removing or exporting a package to another project, it is always good to know all of the package dependencies. Dependency analysis allows users to detect cyclic dependencies that impede reusing the package in the current or other projects because it can cause an infinite recursion. This analysis manages the impact of changes to other related elements.

In the modeling tool, the Dependency Checker allows you to analyze package dependencies in the whole project scope or analyze dependencies between the selected package/model and shared packages. The dependency severity level status shows how a package depends on a particular element. There are three severity levels:

- Info . The lowest severity.
- Warning . Conflicts that require your attention are displayed.
- Error . The highest severity.

#### NOTE: Severity levels

Severity levels

While checking dependencies, conflicts of the selectedseverity (and higher) are displayed in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Package Dependencies panel' space='']**Package Dependencies** panel.

In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Setting project options' space='']**Project Options** dialog, you can define the default values for checking dependencies. You can specify the following:

- The Check for Cyclic Dependencies among Used Projects option. Click to select true if you want the cyclic dependencies to be checked while exporting or sharing the packages.
- The Dependency Checker Severity Level option. Specify the severity level. The dependencies of the specified severity (and higher) will be displayed.
- The Ignore Standard/System Profiles option. Click to select true if you want to exclude the standard/system profiles from checking for cyclic dependencies in used projects.

Each time you start the dependency checking, you can change the default settings.

In the following procedures, learn how to work with the Dependency Checker:

- 
- 
- 
- 

setDefaultTo set default values for the Dependency Checker

1. On the main menu, click Options > Project . The Project Options dialog opens.
2. Expand the General option in the option group list.
3. Click the **Dependency Checker** option. The list of options opens.
4. In the Dependency Checker options list, set the desired values and click **OK** when you are done.

#### NOTE: Description of an option

Description of an option

Each option has a short description. Click the option to see the description in the description area of the dialog.

analyzeWholeTo analyze package dependencies of the whole project or between the selected package/model and shared packages

1. Choose one of the following:
  - To analyze package dependencies of the whole project, on the main menu, click **Analyze** > **Dependency Checker**.
  - To analyze dependencies between the selected package/model and shared packages, right-click the package or model in the Model Browser or on the diagram pane, point to **Tools**and then click**Dependency Checker**.
2. The Dependency Checker dialog opens. Select the Minimal Severity from the list, check ignore or not standard and system profiles while checking and click OK to execute the dependency checker. If some dependencies are found, they are displayed in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Package Dependencies panel' space='']**Package Dependencies** panel .

analyzeExportTo identify package dependencies while exporting packages

1. From the package/model shortcut menu, choose Project Usages > Export Packages to New Project . The Export Packages to New Project dialog appears.
2. Select the packages you want to export. For more information, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Exporting packages to new projects' space=''] .
3. Click OK .
4. In the question dialog, click **Yes**. NotesDependencies between the selected package/model and shared packages will be analyzed and shown in the opened[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Package Dependencies panel' space='']**Package Dependency** panel.If you want to discover *cyclic dependencies*, select the **Check for cyclic dependencies among used projects** check box. This enables for the dependencies which have **Error** and **Warning** severity levels to be displayed.

analyzeSharingTo identify package dependencies while sharing packages

1. From the package/model shortcut menu, choose Project Usages > Share Packages . The Shared Packages dialog appears.
2. Select the packages you want to share. For more information, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sharing project data' space=''].
3. Click OK .
4. In the question dialog, click **Yes**. NotesDependencies between the selected package/model and shared packages will be analyzed and shown in the opened [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Package Dependencies panel' space='']**Package Dependency** panel.If you want to discover *cyclic dependencies*, select the **Check for cyclic dependencies among used projects** check box. This enables for the dependencies which have**Error**and**Warning**severity levels to be displayed.

#### INFO: Cyclic dependency

Cyclic dependency

If there is a chain of dependencies such that A → M(1), M(1) → M(2), M(2) → M(3), ..., M(X) → M(A), where:

- A is an element from the used project M(A)
- M(1..X) are other used projects
- A → M(x) is element A dependency on used project M(x)
- M(y) → M(x) is a dependency of at least one element in used project M(y) on used project M(x),

then this chain is called a cyclic dependency and every atomic dependency in this chain is considered as part of cyclic dependency.

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>In a model, <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Package" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[packages]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> can be associated with other packages or model elements in a project scope or outside the project scope. In other words, packages can have relations with external elements in used projects, shared libraries, profiles, etc. An element depends on the used project when at least one of its metaproperties reference elements from at least one share of that used project. In this case, the element depends on that used project.</p><p>To avoid any violations on editing a package, especially on removing or exporting a package to another project, it is always good to know all of the package dependencies. Dependency analysis allows users to detect cyclic dependencies that impede reusing the package in the current or other projects because it can cause an infinite recursion. This analysis manages the impact of changes to other related elements.</p><p>In the modeling tool, the Dependency Checker allows you to analyze package dependencies in the whole project scope or analyze dependencies between the selected package/model and shared packages. The dependency severity level status shows how a package depends on a particular element. There are three severity levels:</p><ul><li><strong>Info</strong>. The lowest severity. </li><li><strong>Warning</strong>. Conflicts that require your attention are displayed.</li><li><strong>Error</strong>. The highest severity. </li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b03887e5-b308-4bcd-8fcf-2af99096782f"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Severity levels</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>While checking dependencies, conflicts of the selec<span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">ted</span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(255,0,0);"> </span></span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">severity (a</span>nd higher) are displayed in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Package Dependencies panel" /><ac:link-body><strong>Package Dependencies</strong> panel</ac:link-body></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>In the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Setting project options" /><ac:link-body><strong>Project Options</strong> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link>, you can define the default values for checking dependencies. You can specify the following:</p><ul><li>The <strong>Check for Cyclic Dependencies among Used Projects</strong> option. Click to select <em>true</em> if you want the cyclic dependencies to be checked while exporting or sharing the packages.</li><li>The <strong>Dependency Checker Severity Level</strong> option. Specify the severity level. The dependencies of the specified severity (and higher) will be displayed.</li><li>The <strong>Ignore Standard/System Profiles</strong> option. Click to select <em>true</em> if you want to exclude the standard/system profiles from checking for cyclic dependencies in used projects.</li></ul><p>Each time you start the dependency checking, you can change the default settings.</p><p>In the following procedures, learn how to work with the Dependency Checker:</p><ul><li><ac:link ac:anchor="setDefault"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[How to set default values for the Dependency Checker]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link ac:anchor="analyzeWhole"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[How to analyze package dependencies of the whole project or between the selected package/model and shared packages]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link ac:anchor="analyzeExport"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[How to analyze package dependencies while exporting packages]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link ac:anchor="analyzeSharing"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[How to analyze package dependencies while sharing packages]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="60e04eb5-bf0e-481b-80d4-d0f607991a55"><ac:parameter ac:name="">setDefault</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>To set default values for the Dependency Checker</p><hr /><ol><li>On the main menu, click <strong>Options</strong> &gt; <strong>Project</strong>. The <strong>Project Options</strong> dialog opens.</li><li><span>Expand the </span><strong>General</strong><span> option in the option group list.</span></li><li><span><span>Click the <strong>Dependency Checker</strong> option. The list of options opens.</span></span></li><li><span><span>In the Dependency Checker options list, set the desired values and click <strong>OK</strong> when you are done. </span></span><br /><span><span><br /></span></span></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6331d8e7-b12b-4819-be61-16929d96f31a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Description of an option</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Each option has a short description. Click the option to see the description in the description area of the dialog.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color: rgb(255,0,0);"><br /></span></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f0c22bfb-bfa0-4e26-8fff-ea26299092c6"><ac:parameter ac:name="">analyzeWhole</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>To analyze package dependencies of the whole project or between the selected package/model and shared packages</p><hr /><ol><li>Choose one of the following:<ul><li><p>To analyze package dependencies of the whole project, on the main menu, click <strong>Analyze</strong> &gt; <strong>Dependency Checker</strong>.</p></li><li><p>To analyze dependencies between the selected package/model and shared packages, right-click the package or model in the Model Browser or on the diagram pane, point to <strong>Tools </strong>and then click<strong> Dependency Checker</strong>.</p></li></ul></li><li>The <strong>Dependency Checker</strong> dialog opens. Select the <strong>Minimal Severity</strong> from the list, check ignore or not standard and system profiles while checking and click <strong>OK </strong>to execute the dependency checker. If some dependencies are found, they are displayed in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Package Dependencies panel" /><ac:link-body><strong>Package Dependencies</strong> panel</ac:link-body></ac:link>.</li></ol><h4><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><br /></span></h4><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a6fbddf9-c38f-4e51-b775-d1375adca93e"><ac:parameter ac:name="">analyzeExport</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>To identify package dependencies while exporting packages</span></p><hr /><ol><li>From the package/model shortcut menu, choose <strong>Project Usages</strong> &gt; <strong>Export Packages to New Project</strong>. The <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">Export Packages to New Project</strong> dialog appears.</li><li>Select the packages you want to export. For more information, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Exporting packages to new projects" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Exporting packages to new projects]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.</li><li><p>In the question dialog, click <strong>Yes</strong>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4ed83e0a-d035-4270-bf19-7144a508c3e6"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Notes</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Dependencies between the selected package/model and shared packages will be analyzed and shown in the opened<ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Package Dependencies panel" /><ac:link-body> <strong>Package Dependency</strong> panel</ac:link-body></ac:link>.</li><li>If you want to discover <em>cyclic dependencies</em>, select the <strong>Check for cyclic dependencies among used projects</strong> check box. This enables for the dependencies which have <strong>Error</strong> and <strong>Warning</strong> severity levels to be displayed.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5f07b3ff-006b-4bd1-9311-449cc930d658"><ac:parameter ac:name="">analyzeSharing</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>To identify package dependencies while sharing packages</span></p><hr /><ol><li>From the package/model shortcut menu, choose <strong>Project Usages</strong> &gt; <strong>Share Packages</strong>. The <strong>Shared Packages</strong> dialog appears. </li><li><p>Select the packages you want to share. For more information, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Sharing project data" /></ac:link>.</p></li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.</li><li><p>In the question dialog, click <strong>Yes</strong>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3dbff1aa-82b9-49bf-8f0f-67fd53c9331d"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Notes</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Dependencies between the selected package/model and shared packages will be analyzed and shown in the opened <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Package Dependencies panel" /><ac:link-body><strong>Package Dependency</strong> panel</ac:link-body></ac:link>.</li><li>If you want to discover <em>cyclic dependencies</em>, select the <strong>Check for cyclic dependencies among used projects</strong> check box. <span>This enables for the dependencies which have </span><strong>Error</strong><span> and </span><strong>Warning</strong><span> severity levels to be displayed.</span></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><p> </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="36b8cd71-2b36-479a-aa43-86de22bc15ed"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Cyclic dependency</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If there is a chain of dependencies such that A → M(1), M(1) → M(2), M(2) → M(3), ..., M(X) → M(A), where:</p><ul><li>A is an element from the used project M(A)</li><li>M(1..X) are other used projects</li><li>A → M(x) is element A dependency on used project M(x)</li><li>M(y) → M(x) is a dependency of at least one element in used project M(y) on used project M(x),</li></ul><p>then this chain is called a cyclic dependency and every atomic dependency in this chain is considered as part of cyclic dependency.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f8f1e285-e27f-4a7e-a0fa-517801cea20e"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p> <ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="adb780d4-8338-4982-a517-dcadef4fb11e" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249567392 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05475: Identifying relationships

- page_id: `249567392`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249567392/Identifying+relationships
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T18:33:01.819+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > Data-related modeling > Transformations > ER to SQL (generic / Oracle) transformations
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Identifying relationships are transformed in the same way as in the UML to SQL transformation, that is, the foreign key of the transformation gets to be included into the primary key of the dependent entity (the one at the multiple end of the relationship). The difference in ER to SQL transformation case is that the ER model eliminates guessing, which relationships are identifying and which ones are not. UML to SQL transformation guesses, which UML associations should be identifying, by using a heuristic method - composition associations are treated as identifying (this heuristic is controlled by the **Treat compositions as identifying**transformation property). In ER models, identifying relationships are explicitly marked as such, hence there is no need to guess (See [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Identifying relationships and dependent entities' space=''] that specifies how identifying relationships is modeled).

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Identifying relationships are transformed in the same way as in the UML to SQL transformation, that is, the foreign key of the transformation gets to be included into the primary key of the dependent entity (the one at the multiple end of the relationship). The difference in ER to SQL transformation case is that the ER model eliminates guessing, which relationships are identifying and which ones are not. UML to SQL transformation guesses, which UML associations should be identifying, by using a heuristic method - composition associations are treated as identifying (this heuristic is controlled by the <strong>Treat compositions as identifying </strong>transformation property). In ER models, identifying relationships are explicitly marked as such, hence there is no need to guess (See <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Identifying relationships and dependent entities" /></ac:link> that specifies how identifying relationships is modeled).</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249567284 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05476: Identifying relationships and dependent entities

- page_id: `249567284`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249567284/Identifying+relationships+and+dependent+entities
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-18T18:26:53.375+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > Data-related modeling > Entity-relationship (ER) modeling and diagrams
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

One-to-many and (very rarely) one-to-one relationships can be declared as “identifying” relationships. An identifying relationship is a “stronger” version of the relationship, indicating that the entity at the multiple end of the relationship cannot exist without the entity on the other end.

You can create these relationships using the buttons on the diagram pallet, as well as turn an existing relationship into an identifying one and back again. To do this, either change the **Is Identifying**property value in the relationship Specification window or select the appropriate check box on its shortcut menu.

An identifying relationship is drawn as a solid line. Non-identifying relationships use heavy dashes.

Dependent / independent entities are a closely related concept. Dependent entities occur at the multiple end of the identifying relationship. They cannot exist without the independent entity at the other end. In addition, every inherited entity (if you are doing ER modeling) is considered to be dependent. A dependent entity's primary key includes the other entity's key as part. This is implied, not shown in the model. Dependent entities are automatically recognized and drawn with rounded corners.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of identifying relationship and dependent entity in the ER diagram.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>One-to-many and (very rarely) one-to-one relationships can be declared as “identifying” relationships. An identifying relationship is a “stronger” version of the relationship, indicating that the entity at the multiple end of the relationship cannot exist without the entity on the other end.</p><p>You can create these relationships using the buttons on the diagram pallet, as well as turn an existing relationship into an identifying one and back again. To do this, either change the <strong>Is Identifying </strong>property value in the relationship Specification window or select the appropriate check box on its shortcut menu.</p><p>An identifying relationship is drawn as a solid line. Non-identifying relationships use heavy dashes.</p><p>Dependent / independent entities are a closely related concept. Dependent entities occur at the multiple end of the identifying relationship. They cannot exist without the independent entity at the other end. In addition, every inherited entity (if you are doing ER modeling) is considered to be dependent. A dependent entity's primary key includes the other entity's key as part. This is implied, not shown in the model. Dependent entities are automatically recognized and drawn with rounded corners.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2017-9-8_16-26-22.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Example of identifying relationship and dependent entity in the ER diagram.</h6><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243965805 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05477: Image Library tab

- page_id: `243965805`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243965805/Image+Library+tab
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-09-12T13:43:37.906+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > Getting started > Understanding the user interface
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### EXCERPT: Image formats

INLINE

The**Image Library** tab displays images grouped in logical sets. You can use a bundled image collection or add your own set of images.

To open the **Image Library** tab

- From the Window menu, select Image Library. The Image Library tab opens on the right side of the window.

To add your own set of images into **Image Library**, do either

- Compress a folder (.zip) with a set of desired images and place it in the modeling tool's installationdirectory: *\data\imagelibraries\commonsymbols*. Image formatsYou can add images of the following formats: .gif, .jpg, .jpeg, .svg, .png, .wmf.
- Create an Image Library plugin. See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Writing plugins' space='DEVG'] page.

Using the **Image Library** tab, you can [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Applying image' space=''].

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The **Image Library** tab consist of three components:

##### Quick filter

You can search for images in the **Whole hierarchy** or **Recently used** tabs using one of the following way:

- Simple Search: type what you want to find in the hierarchy.
- Pattern Search: type * to find any image. For example, type "*Bank" if you want to find "Bank" somewhere in the hierarchy.

##### Image Library tree

You can drag an image from the **Image Library** tree directly:

- On the symbol (image is applied for symbol).
- To the diagram pane (image shape is created).

##### Image Library Preview area

You can preview the image before applying it on the symbol in the **Image Library** Preview area.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE


<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243968096 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05478: Image Shape

- page_id: `243968096`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243968096/Image+Shape
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:50:50.126+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Auxiliary diagram symbols
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

490233696

490233698

490233697

The image shape provides a simple and quick way to insert a image into a diagram. Images may include logos, graphs, tables, or others. The default shape size after the insertion is the actual image size.

You can format image shape representation properties in the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Formatting symbols' space='']**Symbol Properties**dialog.

To insert an image on the diagram pane

1. On the diagram palette, in the Common category, click the [ATTACHMENT filename='Screen Shot 2017-06-28 at 12.58.27 PM.png'] Image Shape button or press the I key.
2. Click on the diagram pane. The Open dialog opens.
3. Browse for an image in the *.gif, *.jpg, *.jpeg, *.svg, or *.png format and click Open .

490233695

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML elements' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Auxiliary diagram symbols' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Diagramming' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243970816 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05479: Image tool

- page_id: `243970816`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243970816/Image+tool
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:51:59.496+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Report Wizard > Helper tools
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1267909359

1267909360

1267909360

$image provides a rich set of image manipulation methods that enable you to transform images during report generation. With **$image**, you can scale, rotate, split, and resize images, include external images, and export them for TXT and HTML templates.

**Syntax**

These manipulation methods can be broadly divided into seven categories:

1. Scaling images
2. Rotating images
3. Splitting images
4. Resizing fixed-pixel images
5. Resizing fixed-measurement images
6. Including external images
7. Exporting images for TXT and HTML templates
8. Limiting image size

Multiple image transformations will have cumulative effects. Desired sizes can be specified either as pixel counts or as measurements in inches, centimeters, or millimeters. While the parameters for resizing an image with a specific measurement (in, cm, mm, pt, or px) must be specified in a pair of quote marks, all other size parameters do not need to be specified in a pair of quote marks.

The **keepRatio** parameter takes either a true or false value; a true value will resize the image and keep the original image height/width ratio; a false value will only resize one axis, as set by the method name, resulting in a stretched image.

1267909358

**Related pages**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9b7748bf-aafa-4ee5-9b5f-577c3e71bcc3"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1267909359</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1fba6b16-be20-4cae-9695-8a3e27370e49"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1267909360</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e47fa39e-e65c-473b-a896-fc6c2eb833d9"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1267909360</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>$image provides a rich set of image manipulation methods that enable you to transform images during report generation. With <strong>$image</strong>, you can scale, rotate, split, and resize images, include external images, and export them for TXT and HTML templates.</p><p><strong>Syntax</strong></p><p>These manipulation methods can be broadly divided into seven categories:</p><ol><li>Scaling images</li><li>Rotating images</li><li>Splitting images</li><li>Resizing fixed-pixel images</li><li>Resizing fixed-measurement images</li><li>Including external images</li><li>Exporting images for TXT and HTML templates</li><li>Limiting image size</li></ol><p>Multiple image transformations will have cumulative effects. Desired sizes can be specified either as pixel counts or as measurements in inches, centimeters, or millimeters. While the parameters for resizing an image with a specific measurement (in, cm, mm, pt, or px) must be specified in a pair of quote marks, all other size parameters do not need to be specified in a pair of quote marks.</p><p>The <strong>keepRatio</strong> parameter takes either a true or false value; a true value will resize the image and keep the original image height/width ratio; a false value will only resize one axis, as set by the method name, resulting in a stretched image.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f0e833e7-926f-4a24-bee8-36421e59fb28"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1267909358</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="b0bb516e-26b0-4363-a404-fddac23bdb9f" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249565264 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05480: Impact analysis relation maps

- page_id: `249565264`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249565264/Impact+analysis+relation+maps
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-18T16:25:36.506+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > Procedures > Managing traceability and impact analysis
- labels: ['impact-analysis-relation-maps', 'node-role-impact-analysis-map', 'node-impact-analysis-map', 'resource-role-impact-analysis-map', 'resource-impact-analysis-map', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

216907718

216907721

216907719

Take your model to the next steps - perform the impact analysis. For this purpose, there are four predefined relation maps. Using these maps, you are able to identify the model elements which are affected by the changes of the context element.

The predefined relation maps for the impact analysis are the following:

- Operational Role Impact Analysis Map
- Operational Performer Impact Analysis Map
- Resource Role Impact Analysis Map
- Resource Impact Analysis Map

Several predefined criteria helps you to determine how the context element is related to other model elements.

The Legend in the right-top corner shows you which color represents which criteria.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

216907717

**Related procedures**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating predefined relation map' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Modifying criteria through the legend' space='']

For this purpose, there are four predefined relation maps. Using these maps, you are able to identify the model elements which are affected by the changes of the context element.</p><p>The predefined relation maps for the impact analysis are the following:</p><ul><li>Operational Role Impact Analysis Map</li><li>Operational Performer Impact Analysis Map</li><li>Resource Role Impact Analysis Map</li><li>Resource Impact Analysis Map</li></ul><p>Several predefined criteria helps you to determine how the context element is related to other model elements.</p><p>The Legend in the right-top corner shows you which color represents which criteria.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="Resource Impact Analysis Map" ac:alt="Resource Impact Analysis Map"><ri:attachment ri:filename="resource_impact_analysis_map.png" /></ac:image></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="10d714cc-b6c3-4ed3-ba1b-062ed190b0a7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">216907717</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related procedures</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating predefined relation map" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Modifying criteria through the legend" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

## PAGE 05481: Impact Matrix

- page_id: `249565168`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249565168/Impact+Matrix
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T16:59:49.840+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > Procedures
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1335007736

1335007764

1335007753

###### Description

The Impact Matrix describes the mapping between the Strategic/Operational/Resource/Service Elements and Strategic Elements using the Impacted By relationship directly or using the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Implied Relations' space=''].

###### Implementation

The rows of the matrix are:

- Operational Elements (Operational Activity and Operational Architecture)
- Resources Elements (Resource Architecture)
- Services Elements (Service Architecture)
- Strategic Elements (Actual Outcome, Capability, and Actual Strategic Phase)

The columns of the matrix are:

- Strategic Elements (Challenge, Opportunity, and Enterprise Goal)

###### Sample

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

1335007735

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Impact Matrix' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249565173 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05482: Implementation Matrix

- page_id: `249565173`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249565173/Implementation+Matrix
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T16:59:50.205+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > Procedures
- labels: ['implementation-matrix', 'mapping-between-systems-operational-and-service-elements', 'nodes/performers-implementation-matrix', 'operational-activities-implementation-matrix', 'operational-exchanges-implementation-matrix', 'ov-sv-gap-analysis', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1370353206

1370353209

1370353207

**Description**

The **Implementation Matrix** describes the mapping between the Resources / Operational / Summary & Overview Elements and Operational / Services / Strategic / Resources Elementsdirectly or using the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Implied Relations' space=''].The implementation in UAF is defined by the Implements relationship. To specify the implementation and analyze implementation gaps, the Implementation Matrix is added as a supportive product for UAF.

**Implementation**

An Implementation Matrix is represented by a Dependency Matrix-based diagram.

The Rows of the matrix are:

- Resources Elements: Resource Performer, Function, Resource Interfaces, Resource Information, Resource Role, Resource Service, Resource Connector, and Resource Exchange.
- Operational Elements: Operational Activity and Operational Information.
- Service Elements: Service Interface.
- Summary & Overview Elements: Architecture.

The Columns of this matrix are:

- Operational Elements: Operational Agent, Operational Activity, Operational Interface, Operational Information, Operational Role, Operational Connector, and Operational Exchange.
- Services Elements: Service, Service Function, and Service Interface.
- Strategic Elements: Actual Strategic Phase and Strategic Information.
- Resource Elements: Resource Connector.

**Sample**

**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

**Predefined configurations**

The predefined matrices are only available in DoDAF and DoDAF 2.0 perspectives.

Working in DoDAF or DoDAF 2.0 perspectives, you can find predefined matrices by clicking **Analyze** > **OV-SV Gap analysis**.

There are several types of predefined implementation matrices:

- Operational Performers Implementation Matrix. It maps Operational Performers to System Resources only.
- Operational Activities Implementation Matrix. It maps Operational Activities to Functions.
- Operational Exchanges Implementation Matrix. It maps Operational Exchanges to Resource Exchanges.

These matrices provide an analysis of the whole model of a particular implementation, so you do not have to define the scope or any additional properties for building them.

1370353205

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='System in UAF' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Function' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Resource Exchange' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Performer' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Activity' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Exchange' space='MED']

**Related procedures**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Implied Relations' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243968731 space=MT version=6 -->
## PAGE 05483: Implementing or Overriding Operations

- page_id: `243968731`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243968731/Implementing+or+Overriding+Operations
- version_number: 6
- version_date: `2025-09-12T13:28:42.591+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Tools
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### NOTE: Modeling Tools

Modeling Tools

This functionality is available in Standard, Professional, Architect and Enterprise editions only.

When you inherit Classes from the base Class that has abstract functions, you have to redefine them in the inherited Classes. The implement/override operations tool will help you generate operations that are defined as abstract in the base Class.

The**Implement/Override Operations**command can be invoked in two cases:

- When one classifier inherits operations from the base classifier ( [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Case study #1: Adding a rule to create a Generalization relationship on drag-and-drop' space='UPDG'] ).
- When some classifiers implement an Interface ( [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Forward traceability - realization' space=''] ).

To start the Implement/Override Operations****tool

1. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='DH Links panel shortcut menu and Sync Status icons' space='CDH'] of the selected Class, select Tools > Code Engineering .
2. Select Implement/Override Operations.
3. The Select Operations to Implement/Override dialog box opens.

| Box | Function |
| --- | --- |
| All | Contains names of all operations defined within the selected class. |
| Selected | Contains the selected operations. |
| > | Moves the selected operation from the All list to the Selected list. |
| < | Moves selected operation from the Selected list to the All list. |
| >> | Moves all operations from the All list to the Selected list. |
| << | Moves all operations from the Selected to the All list. |
| OK | Generates operations that are in the Selected list. |
| Cancel | Exits the dialog box without any changes. |
| Help | Displays help. |

#### TIP: Shortcut Key

Shortcut Key

Double-click the item name and it will be moved to the opposite list.

**Related Pages**

- Tools
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class' space='MED']
- Class diagram
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Case study #1: Adding a rule to create a Generalization relationship on drag-and-drop' space='UPDG']
- Realization relationship

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249561297 space=MT version=5 -->
## PAGE 05484: Implied Connectors

- page_id: `249561297`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249561297/Implied+Connectors
- version_number: 5
- version_date: `2025-09-09T11:01:05.376+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > SysML v1 modeling > Modeling structure with Blocks > Connecting Blocks in SysML Internal Block Diagram
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The implied relation functionality allows you to analyze existing relations among parts in the Internal Block Diagrams. It let you know that parts are somehow related with each other without giving the detailed view of actual Connectors and ports. You can display implied relations between parts which are connected through ports/interfaces. By default, the implied relation is represented as a dashed line with a short summary in label of how many actual connectors are underneath. You can specify a different style of the implied relation by using its the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Symbol Properties dialog' space='']**Symbol Properties** dialog.

The implied relation functionality allows you to:

- Display implied relations between [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Part Property' space='MED'] through ports/interfaces.
- Display actual [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Connector' space='MED'] and ports from the implied relations level by level or all at once.
- Find actual Connectors in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Containment tab' space=''] directly from the implied relation.
- Specify [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Symbol Properties dialog' space=''] of the implied relation.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### On the left side: the implied relation shows that 18 connectors exist between TV and TV Tuner parts. On the right side: all 18 actual connectors and ports are shown by using the Semantic Zoom In, or Display Underlying Connectors command.

How to work with implied Connectors read in the following pages:

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='SysML Internal Block Diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Connecting parts through interface' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Hiding direction arrow on port shape' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying parts and ports in SysML v1' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Autowire Parts' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Property path notation' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Enforce Ports Compatibility mode' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying elements' space='']

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Full Port' space='MED']

**Sample model**

The sample model used in the figures on this page is the **Implied Connectors**sample model. Download [ATTACHMENT filename='Implied Connectors.mdzip'].

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>The implied relation functionality allows you to analyze existing relations among parts in the Internal Block Diagrams. It let you know that parts are somehow related with each other without giving the detailed view of actual Connectors and ports. You can display implied relations between parts which are connected through ports/interfaces. By default, the implied relation is represented as a dashed line with a short summary in label of how many actual connectors are underneath. You can specify a different style of the implied relation by using its the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Symbol Properties dialog" /><ac:link-body><strong>Symbol Properties</strong> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link>.</p><p>The implied relation functionality allows you to:</p><ul><li data-uuid="f42f4001-7636-4308-abe0-6ad6eb793d77">Display implied relations between <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Part Property" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Part Properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> <s> </s>through ports/interfaces.</li><li data-uuid="a901c316-744f-480e-97e6-0a52ddd14060">Display actual <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Connector" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Connectors]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and ports from the implied relations level by level or all at once.</li><li data-uuid="832eec36-6b37-41a5-8d25-462c30370897">Find actual Connectors in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Containment tab" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Containment tree]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> directly from the implied relation.</li><li data-uuid="c268a5d7-6630-49ff-96c4-53d0d89eb655">Specify <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Symbol Properties dialog" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[symbol properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> of the implied relation.</li></ul><p><span style="color:var(--ds-background-accent-red-bolder,#c9372c);"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="implied_relations_zoom_in.png" /></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">On the left side: the implied relation shows that 18 connectors exist between TV and TV Tuner parts. On the right side: all 18 actual connectors and ports are shown by using the Semantic Zoom In, or Display Underlying Connectors command.</h6><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">How to work with implied Connectors read in the following pages:</span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="7e1419f1-df1f-448f-92b2-d1448b471e51" /></span></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li class="with-breadcrumbs" data-uuid="2e5e9793-bce6-4f92-9732-0105143b9314"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="SysML Internal Block Diagram" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="be6e77aa-bf49-44bc-bf27-16fdc39124f6"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Connecting parts through interface" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="f20cc86a-3558-451a-a0ce-dfb7c0c73ced"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Displaying parts and ports in SysML v1" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="54826777-7089-48f9-8dda-890c0f8eb755"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Autowire Parts" /></ac:link></li><li data-uuid="fbcad5c2-ec21-40df-8f04-1ae4dce6fab2"><ac:link><ri:page <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249567589 space=MT version=1 -->
````
## PAGE 05485: Import

- page_id: `249567589`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249567589/Import
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-18T18:31:03.672+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > Data-related modeling > XML schemas > XML schema mapping to UML elements
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Maps to a UML Permission with the stereotype XSDimport. The permission client must be the schema class stereotypes*«XSDschema*» Component, supplier namespace Package *XSDnamespace*.

- namespace maps to the supplier name.
- annotation maps to the UML Attribute documentation.
- schemaLocation maps to the TaggedValue.

#### PANEL: import XML representation summary

import XML representation summary

```text
<import
```

```text
id = ID
```

```text
namespace = anyURI
```

```text
schemaLocation = anyURI
```

```text
{any attributes with non-schema namespace…}>	Content: (annotation?)
```

```text
</import>
```

In the following example, the same namespace may be used both for real work, and in the course of defining schema components in terms of foreign components.

#### PANEL: The treatment of references as internal references to the names being defined in a schema document

The treatment of references as internal references to the names being defined in a schema document

```text
<schema xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema" xmlns:html="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml" targetNamespace="uri:mywork" xmlns:my="uri:mywork">
```

```text
<import namespace="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml"/>
```

```text
<annotation>
```

```text
<documentation>
```

```text
<html:p>[Some documentation for my schema]</html:p>		</documentation>
```

```text
</annotation>
```

```text
…
```

```text
<complexType name="myType">
```

```text
<sequence>
```

```text
<element ref="html:p" minOccurs="0"/>		</sequence>
```

```text
…
```

```text
</complexType>
```

```text
<element name="myElt" type="my:myType"/>
```

```text
</schema>
```

The treatment of references as ·**QNames**· implies that since the target namespace and the XML Schema namespace differ (with the exception of the schema for schemas), then without massive redeclaration of the default namespace either the internal references to the names being defined in a schema document or the schema declaration and definition elements themselves must be explicitly qualified. This example shows the first option, while most other examples in this specification showed the second.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']import UML model example.

#### PANEL: import XML code sample

import XML code sample

```text
<xs:schema xmlns:nm = "http://nomagic.com" xmlns:xs = "http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema" targetNamespace = "http://nomagic.com"›
```

```text
<xs:import namespace = "http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml" schemaLocation = "http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml" />
```

```text
</xs:schema>
```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE


<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243968155 space=MT version=4 -->
## PAGE 05487: Import Maps migration from CSV Import Plugin

- page_id: `243968155`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243968155/Import+Maps+migration+from+CSV+Import+Plugin
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-09-10T12:05:47.087+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Working with model elements > Importing data from Excel and CSV files
- labels: ['import-map-migration', 'show-excel-csv-import-migration-message', 'csv-import-map-migration', 'msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This page is for those who used the CSV Import Plugin to import data into the model. As of the 2021x version, this plugin is deprecated and import functionality is covered by [Excel/CSV Sync](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Sync+with+Excel+or+CSV+files) (import to table) and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Importing data from Excel and CSV files' space=''] (import to model) features.

All Import Maps or Map Groups created with the CSV Import Plugin can be reused by migrating them. The procedure outlined below shows how to import those maps:

To import data from the Import Maps or Map Groups created with the CSV Import Plugin

1. Open the project containing the Import Maps/Map Groups created with the CSV Import Plugin.
2. In the top left corner of the modeling tool, click the File > Import From > Excel/CSV File > Import Using Existing Map**** or Import Using Existing Map Group .
3. In the **CSV Import Map Migration** message, select **Yes**. To perform the migration of Import Maps or Map Groups later, make sure the **Do not ask again** check box is clear.
4. In the **Select Import Map** or **Select Map Group**dialog, select Import Map or Map Group.
5. Click OK . The **Excel/CSV Import** dialog opens.
6. Select the file, check mapping options. How to >>
7. Click the **Import** button. 
The data is imported. A Map Group is migrated only if all Import Maps belonging to that Map Group are migrated at the same time. If at least one is not migrated, the whole Map Group will not be migrated.

If the **CSV Import Map Migration** message does not show again when trying to migrate the Import Map or Map Group, you can enable this option in the **Project Options** dialog. For this, follow the procedure below.

To enable the **CSV Import Map Migration** message

1. From the main menu, select Options > Project .
2. In the search box of the Project Options dialog, type " mig ".
3. On the right side of the Project Options dialog, set the Show Excel/CSV Import Migration Message option value to true to enable the functionality.
4. Click OK .

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>This page is for those who used the CSV Import Plugin to import data into the model. As of the 2021x version, this plugin is deprecated and import functionality is covered by <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Sync+with+Excel+or+CSV+files">Excel/CSV Sync</a> (import to table) and <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Importing data from Excel and CSV files" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Excel/CSV Import]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> (import to model) features.</p><p>All Import Maps or Map Groups created with the CSV Import Plugin can be reused by migrating them. The procedure outlined below shows how to import those maps:</p><p><br /></p><p>To import data from the Import Maps or Map Groups created with the CSV Import Plugin</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the project containing the Import Maps/Map Groups created with the CSV Import Plugin.</li><li>In the top left corner of the modeling tool, click the <strong>File</strong> &gt; <strong>Import From</strong> &gt; <strong>Excel/CSV File &gt; Import Using Existing Map<strong> </strong></strong>or <strong>Import Using Existing Map Group</strong>.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the <strong>CSV Import Map Migration</strong> message, select <strong>Yes</strong>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d34b7df2-dfb8-43dd-aa7f-895a7782a5b7"><ac:rich-text-body><p>To perform the migration of Import Maps or Map Groups later, make sure the <strong>Do not ask again</strong> check box is clear.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the <strong>Select Import Map</strong> or <strong>Select Map Group </strong>dialog, select Import Map or Map Group. </p></li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>. The <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Excel+and+CSV+Import+dialog"><strong>Excel/CSV Import</strong> dialog</a> opens. </li><li>Select the file, check mapping options. <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Excel+and+CSV+Import+dialog#ExcelandCSVImportdialog-SelectExcel/CSVfilearea" rel="nofollow">How to &gt;&gt;</a></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Click the <strong>Import</strong> button.<br />The data is imported.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="903c2d06-1978-4902-a2a1-54dc3404e22c"><ac:rich-text-body><p>A Map Group is migrated only if all Import Maps belonging to that Map Group are migrated at the same time. If at least one is not migrated, the whole Map Group will not be migrated.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /><br /></p></li></ol><p>If the <strong>CSV Import Map Migration</strong> message does not show again when trying to migrate the Import Map or Map Group, you can enable this option in the <strong>Project Options</strong> dialog. For this, follow the procedure below.</p><p><br /></p><p>To enable the <strong>CSV Import Map Migration</strong> message</p><hr /><ol><li>From the main menu, select <strong>Options</strong> &gt; <strong>Project</strong>.</li><li>In the search box of the <strong>Project Options</strong> dialog, type &quot;<em>mig</em>&quot;.</li><li>On the right side of the <strong>Project Options</strong> dialog, set the <strong>Show Excel/CSV Import Migration Message </strong>option value to <em>true</em> to enable the functionality.</li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.</li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243971203 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05488: Import syntax

- page_id: `243971203`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243971203/Import+syntax
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-07T17:24:26.507+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Report Wizard > Import tool
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Information

715763235

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Information

715763237

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Information

715763236

To use the Import tool syntax

1. To declare Import tool in the template, type #import ('import', 'com.nomagic.reportwizard.tools.ImportTool') in the document that you want to import its content to the report. The #import directive will load Import tool so that it can be accessed inside the template.
  - The first parameter 'import' is for identifying the alias of Import tool (in this case, 'import' , but it can be named anything).
  - The second parameter 'com.nomagic.reportwizard.tools.ImportTool' has to be copied as it is (do not rename it). Otherwise, Import tool will not be loaded.
2. To import any content from a child template, type the following in the document that you want to import its content to the report:

- $import.include('child.docx') to import a complete document at the current position of the template, and/or
- $import.includeSection('child.docx', 'Section A') to include only the text contained in the named section of the document.

****

**$import**

This part names the alias assigned to Import tool when declaring it to the template.

****

**$import.include(FileName)**

**FileName** in [$import.include(FileName)](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Import+usage#Importusage-UsageinExample1) specifies the location of a file in the file system. **FileName** can be either an absolute or relative path to a file in the file system. The **FileName** parameter can be set either statically or dynamically. If the file is not found in the file system, **FileName** would be searched as an Attached File element in a project in the **File** property.

****

**$import.includeSection(FileName, SectionName)**

**FileName** in [$import.includeSection(FileName, SectionName)](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Import+usage#Importusage-UsageinExample2) functions the same as in the sub-section above. **FileName** can be either an absolute or relative path to a file in the file system. **SectionName** denotes a paragraph in the document named **FileName**, which is delimited by the **#sectionBegin(SectionName)** and **#sectionEnd** identifiers. The **SectionName** variable can also be set either statically or dynamically. If the file is not found in the file system, **FileName** would be searched as an Attached File element in a project in the **File** property.

#### INFO: Information

Information

You can also use **$attachedFile**in the parameter of **$import.include(FileName)** and **$import.includeSection(FileName, SectionName)**.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3207e9a8-b8f7-45e5-82d3-f4e215d37b1d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">715763235</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="33426169-fbae-4ee7-ae1b-ff54e4249423"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">715763237</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7d1e5f3f-4b1e-4e4c-a5da-a0bab2d38a7d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">715763236</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>To use the Import tool syntax</p><hr /><ol><li>To declare Import tool in the template, type <strong>#import ('import', 'com.nomagic.reportwizard.tools.ImportTool')</strong> in the document that you want to import its content to the report. The <strong>#import</strong> directive will load Import tool so that it can be accessed inside the template.<br /><ul><li>The first parameter<strong> 'import'</strong> is for identifying the alias of Import tool (in this case, <strong>'import'</strong>, but it can be named anything).</li><li>The second parameter <strong>'com.nomagic.reportwizard.tools.ImportTool'</strong> has to be copied as it is (do not rename it). Otherwise, Import tool will not be loaded.</li></ul></li><li>To import any content from a child template, type the following in the document that you want to import its content to the report:    </li></ol><ul><li style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><strong>$import.include('child.docx')</strong> to import a complete document at the current position of the template, and/or</li><li style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><strong>$import.includeSection('child.docx', 'Section A')</strong> to include only the text contained in the named section of the document.</li></ul><p><strong><br /></strong></p><p><strong>$import</strong></p><p>This part names the alias assigned to Import tool when declaring it to the template.</p><p><strong><br /></strong></p><p><strong>$import.include(FileName)</strong></p><p><strong>FileName</strong> in <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Import+usage#Importusage-UsageinExample1">$import.include(FileName)</a> specifies the location of a file in the file system. <strong>FileName</strong> can be either an absolute or relative path to a file in the file system. The <strong>FileName</strong> parameter can be set either statically or dynamically. If the file is not found in the file system, <strong>FileName</strong> would be searched as an Attached File element in a project in the <strong>File</strong> property.</p><p><strong><br /></strong></p><p><strong>$import.includeSection(FileName, SectionName)</strong></p><p><strong>FileName</strong> in <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Import+usage#Importusage-UsageinExample2">$import.includeSection(FileName, SectionName)</a> functions the same as in the sub-section above. <strong>FileName</strong> can be either an absolute or relative path to a file in the file system. <strong>SectionName</strong> denotes a paragraph in the document named <strong>FileName</strong>, which is delimited by the <strong>#sectionBegin(SectionName)</strong> and <strong>#sectionEnd</strong> identifiers. The <strong>SectionName</strong> variable can also be set either statically or dynamically. If the file is not found in the file system, <strong>FileName</strong> would be searched as an Attached File element in a project in the <strong>File</strong> property.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="dd51469a-e548-4f4f-83ef-ac7e3af3ab2e"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Information</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can also use <strong>$attachedFile </strong>in the parameter of <strong>$import.include(FileName)</strong> and <strong>$import.includeSection(FileName, SectionName)</strong>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243971202 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05489: Import tool

- page_id: `243971202`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243971202/Import+tool
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:52:07.985+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Report Wizard
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1175109811

1175109813

1175109812

Import tool is a module of functions that allows importing documents to be in generated reports. Import tool enables you to dynamically import whole or parts of documents into reports, giving you greater flexibility when generating reports that require dynamic resources. You can now include documents whose location is only known at the actual translation time.

The Import tool allows you to:

- Parse and import RTF , DOCX, HTML , and text templates from any location in the file system
- Reuse the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Report Wizard custom language' space=''] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Report Wizard custom language' space=''] syntax to import any part of a document. The Import tool uses the predefined syntaxes #sectionBegin and #sectionEnd , introduced with the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Report Wizard custom language' space=''] .

1175109810

**Related pages**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="43a306aa-5cf3-487d-b5ab-7dc55be2e4b0"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1175109811</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5906bd29-43fe-4769-ba0e-a0555c875317"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1175109813</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9338f24e-8903-4191-82d2-3e615044c314"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1175109812</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Import tool is a module of functions that allows importing documents to be in generated reports. Import tool enables you to dynamically import whole or parts of documents into reports, giving you greater flexibility when generating reports that require dynamic resources. You can now include documents whose location is only known at the actual translation time.</p><p>The Import tool allows you to:</p><ul><li>Parse and import <em>RTF</em>, <em>DOCX, HTML</em>, and text templates from any location in the file system</li><li>Reuse the <strong><ac:link ac:anchor="#includeSectionDirective"><ri:page ri:content-title="Report Wizard custom language" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[#sectionBegin]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></strong> and <strong><ac:link ac:anchor="#includeSectionDirective"><ri:page ri:content-title="Report Wizard custom language" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[#sectionEnd]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></strong> syntax to import any part of a document. The Import tool uses the predefined syntaxes <strong>#sectionBegin</strong> and <strong>#sectionEnd</strong>, introduced with the <strong><ac:link ac:anchor="#includeSectionDirective"><ri:page ri:content-title="Report Wizard custom language" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[#includeSection Directive]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></strong>.</li></ul><p> </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5c24317f-6306-485b-83a6-79ca6155bab8"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1175109810</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="02c30e2f-93e2-4da8-9cd7-9b065513f43f" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243971204 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05490: Import usage

- page_id: `243971204`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243971204/Import+usage
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:52:08.178+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Report Wizard > Import tool
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This section explains the examples of usage of the main and child templates. These examples allow you to create documents as the main template and reuse the content of child templates into it.

#### Preparatory Step

Define a child template that you will use as in Examples 1, 2, and 3. This child template contains the header line and named sections (**Section A** and **Section B**). Type the following lines in the document that you want to define the child template:

```text

```

#### Usage in Example 1

This example shows how to statically include the complete child template as shown in the Preparatory Step. To include it, call the **$import.include('child.docx')** method. Note that there is no specified path. This path to the child template shows you that the main and child templates reside in the same directory. Type the following lines in the document that you want to include all of its content to the report:

```text

```

The output right below will include the entire text from the main template (right above) and from the child template minus the template directives. The included content will be then parsed and stripped of all velocity directives. The output is as follows:

```text

```

#### Usage in Example 2

This example shows how to statically include a section of the child template as shown in the Preparatory Step. To include the section named **Section A** from the child template, call the **$import.includeSection('templates/child.rtf', 'Section A')** method. In this example, the main and child templates reside in different directories, which means that the child template will reside in a subdirectory called *templates*. This *templates* directory will thus reside in the same directory as the main template. Type the following lines in the document that you want to include its content in **Section A** to the report:

```text

```

The output will be:

```text

```

#### Usage in Example 3

This example shows how to dynamically include the section of a child template as shown in the Preparatory Step. Before dynamically including the section of the child template named **Section B**, set a variable for the file location of the child template **[#set ($child = "***C:/ImportTool/child.rtf***")]** and another one for the section name to be included**[#set ($section = "Section B")]**. To include the **Section B** section from the child template, call the **$import.includeSection($child, $section)** method. In this example, the child's template location is provided by an absolute path. Note that dynamic values must not be specified in a pair of quote marks, otherwise the section will not be included. Type the following lines in the document that you want to include its content in **Section B** to the report:

```text

```

The output will be:

```text

```

The Import tool supports *DOCX, RTF, HTML, XML*, and*text* files only.

#### Usage in Example 4

This example shows how to include a document as an Attached File element in a project. First, you need to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Attaching, reattaching, and detaching files' space=''] element in a project. The **File** property will then be used as a parameter to make a reference to the Attached File element. Type the following lines in the document that you want to include the content of the Attached File element to the report:

```text

```

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Including a document as an Attached File element in a project.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

These examples allow you to create documents as the main template and reuse the content of child templates into it.</p><h2>Preparatory Step</h2><p>Define a child template that you will use as in Examples 1, 2, and 3. This child template contains the header line and named sections (<strong>Section A</strong> and <strong>Section B</strong>). Type the following lines in the document that you want to define the child template:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c5fc47c3-5cca-4eaa-b170-f0b958c676e7"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[This is the child template.
````html
#sectionBegin(Section A)
This is Section A.
#sectionEnd

This is Section B.
#sectionEnd]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h2>Usage in Example 1</h2><p>This example shows how to statically include the complete child template as shown in the Preparatory Step. To include it, call the <strong>$import.include('child.docx')</strong> method. Note that there is no specified path. In this example, the main and child templates reside in different directories, which means that the child template will reside in a subdirectory called <em>templates</em>. Type the following lines in the document that you want to include its content in <strong>Section A</strong> to the report:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3ce50327-1838-4616-b129-4f4d56003298"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#import ('import', 'com.nomagic.reportwizard.tools.ImportTool')

Include Section A
This is Section A.]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h2>Usage in Example 3</h2><p>This example shows how to dynamically include the section of a child template as shown in the Preparatory Step. Before dynamically including the section of the child template named <strong>Section B</strong>, set a variable for the file location of the child template <strong>[#set ($child = &quot;</strong><em>C:/ImportTool/child.rtf</em><strong>&quot;)]</strong> and another one for the section name to be included<strong> [#set ($section = &quot;Section B&quot;)]</strong>. To include the <strong>Section B</strong> section from the child template, call the <strong>$import.includeSection($child, $section)</strong> method. In this example, the child's template location is provided by an absolute path. Note that dynamic values must not be specified in a pair of quote marks, otherwise the section will not be included. Type the following lines in the document that you want to include its content in <strong>Section B</strong> to the report:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d10683bc-cf02-44d9-ab7a-35406d92e52a"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#import ('import', 'com.nomagic.reportwizard.tools.ImportTool')
This is the 3rd main template

$import.includeSection($child, $section)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The output will be:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="be15115f-a695-4e24-9cb6-10022f943c67"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[This is the 3rd main template
Include Section B
This is Section B.]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3e9e5e3b-600a-4d8a-b607-5ba5ccd20fdc"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The Import tool supports <em>DOCX, RTF, HTML, XML</em>, and<em> text</em> files only.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h2>Usage in Example 4</h2><p>This example shows how to include a document as an Attached File element in a project. First, you need to <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Attaching, reattaching, and detaching files" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[create an Attached File]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> element in a project. The <strong>File</strong> property will then be used as a parameter to make a reference to the Attached File element. Type the following lines in the document that you want to include the content of the Attached File element to the report:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="eade2b34-65c8-4540-8454-0bc694a8a8ed"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#import ('import', 'com.nomagic.reportwizard.tools.ImportTool')
#foreach($included in $AttachedFile)

	#end
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Attach File Property.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Including a document as an Attached File element in a project.</h6><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243971312 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05491: Importing a template to modeling tool using Report Wizard

- page_id: `243971312`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243971312/Importing+a+template+to+modeling+tool+using+Report+Wizard
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-07T17:24:31.044+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Report Wizard > Template Creation Tutorial > Creating Your First Template
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1356305390

1356305392

1356305389

**On this page**

3

1356305392

#### Importing a template to the modeling tool

Once you have created a template, you will need to import it into the modeling tool by using Report Wizard.

To import a template into a modeling tool using Report Wizard

1. Click Tools > Report Wizard on the main menu.
2. Click the **New** button in the **Select a report template** wizard. The **New Template** dialog will open. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Opening the Report Wizard dialog and creating a new custom template by clicking the New button.
3. In the **New Template** dialog, type the name and description. Select a category, and then click**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Entering the new template name and description, and then selecting a template file in the New Template dialog.
4. Find the template file that you have created, and click [ATTACHMENT filename='Select_button.png'] .
5. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='Create_button.png'] to import the template.

To generate a report based on a template, select the template (in this example, **My template)**from the template list, and follow the steps as instructed by the wizard. See [Report Wizard User Guide](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Report+Wizard) for more information.

#### Glossary

- Report template Ready-made report configurations that you can use to quickly create a specific type of report in Report Wizard.

#### More information about working with templates and reports

For more information about working with templates and reports, visit the following pages:

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Template Management Pane' space='']

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Default templates' space='']

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Report Data Management Pane' space='']

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Generating Reports from Report Wizard' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

The <strong>New Template</strong> dialog will open.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="newTemplate.png" /></ac:image></p><h6>Opening the Report Wizard dialog and creating a new custom template by clicking the New button.</h6></li><li><p>In the <strong>New Template</strong> dialog, type the name and description.
