See <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Report+Wizard">Report Wizard User Guide</a> for more information.</p><h2>Glossary</h2><ul><li><strong>Report template</strong><br />Ready-made report configurations that you can use to quickly create a specific type of report in Report Wizard.</li></ul><h2>More information about working with templates and reports</h2><p>For more information about working with templates and reports, visit the following pages:</p><p><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Template Management Pane" /></ac:link></p><p><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Default templates" /></ac:link></p><p><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Report Data Management Pane" /></ac:link></p><p><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Generating Reports from Report Wizard" /></ac:link></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243968160 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05492: Importing Blocks and their properties

- page_id: `243968160`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243968160/Importing+Blocks+and+their+properties
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-07T17:23:07.972+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Working with model elements > Importing data from Excel and CSV files > Import examples
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Lets say, we need to import the engine structure including Blocks, their Part and Value Properties. Firstly, open an existing or create new project. In this case, we create new project. Next, do the following:

1. 
2. 
3. 
4. 

###### Importing Blocks

To import Block properties, the an *Engine* and *Engine Type* Blocks must be created in the model. In this case, we import those blocks. For this, in the top-left corner of the modeling tool, click the**File**>**Import From**>**Excel/CSV File > Import Using New Map**. In the [**Excel/CSV Import** dialog](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Excel+and+CSV+Import+dialog) specify the following options:

1. Select the Excel file which contains the spreadsheet with the structure. In this case, the Engine Excel file is selected.
2. Select Block as Element Type option value.
3. Specify the location of imported elements in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Containment Tree' space='MED'] by selecting the Target Scope option value. In this case, the Structure Package is selected.
4. Specify the Excel file sheet in which the table is prepared to import. In this case, the table is in the Sheet 1.
5. Create mapping between element property Name and excel table cell Name.
6. Click Import . The Engine and Engine Type Blocks are created in the model. [ATTACHMENT filename='importing_engine_blocks.png']

###### Saving properties Import Maps

After the Blocks are imported, their all properties can be imported at once by creating a Map Group which consists of 3 Import Maps: *Engine Parts*, *Engine Value Properties*, *Engine Type Value Properties*. Follow the procedures below to save Import Maps.

The *Engine* Block is decomposed to *Engine Type* Part Property. Let's create an Import Map for the Part Property. In the top-left corner of the modeling tool, click the**File**>**Import From**>**Excel/CSV File > Import Using New Map**. In the [**Excel/CSV Import** dialog](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Excel+and+CSV+Import+dialog) specify the following options:

1. Select the Excel file which contains the spreadsheet with the structure. In this case, the Engine Excel file is selected.
2. Select Part Property as Element Type option value.
3. Select the Engine Block as the Target Scope option value.
4. Specify the Excel file sheet in which the table is prepared to import. In this case, the table is in the Sheet 2.
5. Create mapping between element property Name and excel table cell Name.
6. Click Save Map .
7. In the Save Import Map dialog, select a location wherein to save the Import Map. We highly recommend select the Excel/CSV Imports Package.
8. Click the Create button. In the opened Specification window, type the Import Map name - in this case - Engine Part . Click OK > Close . The Engine Part Import Map is saved. [ATTACHMENT filename='saving_engine_parts_import_map.png']

The *Engine* Block contains *serialNum* and *year*Value Properties. To save this Import Map, in the already opened [**Excel/CSV Import** dialog](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Excel+and+CSV+Import+dialog) specify options:

1. Select the Excel file which contains the spreadsheet with value properties. In this case, the Engine Excel file is selected.
2. Select Value Property as Element Type option value.
3. Select Name and Type properties as Properties to Map option value.
4. Select the Engine Block as the Target Scope option value.
5. Specify the Excel file sheet in which the table is prepared to import. In this case, the table is in the Sheet 3.
6. Create mapping between element property Name, Type and excel table cell Name, Type.
7. Select to search for references in entire model, to set types for Value Properties.
8. Click Save Map .
9. In the Save Import Map dialog, select a location wherein to save the Import Map. We highly recommend select the Excel/CSV Imports Package.
10. Click the Create button. In the opened Specification window, type the Import Map name - in this case - Engine Value Properties . Click OK > Close . The Engine Value Properties Import Map is saved.

Also, the E*ngine Type* Block contains *make*, *model*, *hp*, and *fuel***Value Properties. To save this Import Map, in the already opened [**Excel/CSV Import** dialog](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Excel+and+CSV+Import+dialog) specify options:

1. Select the Excel file which contains the spreadsheet with value properties. In this case, the Engine Excel file is selected.
2. Select Value Property as Element Type option value.
3. Select Name and Type properties as Properties to Map option value.
4. Select the Engine Type Block as the Target Scope option value.
5. Specify the Excel file sheet in which the table is prepared to import. In this case, the table is in the Sheet 4.
6. Create mapping between element property Name and excel table cell Name.
7. Select to search for references in entire model, to set types for Value Properties.
8. Click Save Map .
9. In the Save Import Map dialog, select a location wherein to save the Import Map. We highly recommend select the Excel/CSV Imports Package.
10. Click the Create button. In the opened Specification window, type the Import Map name - in this case - Engine Type Value Properties . Click OK > Close . The Engine Type Value Properties Import Map is saved.

###### Creating Map Group

After, the properties Import Maps are prepared, let's create the *Engine Properties Import* Map Group and add the following Import Maps: *Engine Parts*, *Engine Value Properties*, *Engine Type Value Properties.* For this, in the top-left corner of the modeling tool, click the**File**>**Import From**>**Excel/CSV File > Manage Map Groups.**In the **Manage Map Groups** dialog, do the following:

1. In the **Tree** tab, select the *Excel/CSV imports* Package as the owner for the new Map Group.
2. Click the **Create** button.
3. In the Specification window, type thename *Engine Properties Import* for the new Map Group and click **Close**. The newly created Map Group is selected as the Select Map Group for Editing option value.
4. Press Ctrl and in the Tree tab, select the *Engine Parts*, *Engine Value Properties*, *Engine Type Value Properties* Import Maps.
5. C lick [ATTACHMENT filename='add_import_map.png'] to add them to the *Engine Properties Import* Import Map.
6. Click **OK**.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

****

###### Importing Map Group

Finally, import an *Engine Properties Import* Map Group. For this, in the top-left corner of the modeling tool, click the**File**>**Import From**>**Excel/CSV File > Import Using Existing Map Group.**In the **Select Map Group** dialog, select the *Engine Properties Import* Map Group and click **OK**. All properties are created for the *Engine* and *Engine Type* Blocks at once.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Lets say, we need to import the engine structure including Blocks, their Part and Value Properties. Firstly, open an existing or create new project. In this case, we create new project. Next, do the following:</p><ol><li><ac:link ac:anchor="Importing Blocks"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Import Blocks]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link ac:anchor="Saving properties Import Maps"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Save properties Import Maps]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link ac:anchor="Creating Map Group"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Create Map Group]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link ac:anchor="Creating Map Group"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Import Map Group]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ol><h4>Importing Blocks</h4><p>To import Block properties, the an <em>Engine</em> and <em>Engine Type</em> Blocks must be created in the model. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249567306 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05493: Importing CA ERwin® data modeler projects

- page_id: `249567306`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249567306/Importing+CA+ERwin%C2%AE+data+modeler+projects
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-18T18:26:53.833+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > Data-related modeling > Entity-relationship (ER) modeling and diagrams
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1276634702

1276634704

1276634701

**On this page**

33

1276634703

The Cameo Data Modeler Plugin for MagicDraw provides import functionality for data models created using the CA ERwin® Data Modeler (henceforth will be referred as ERwin). ERwin is one of the leaders in the data modeling tools market.

Data models produced in ERwin have a two-layer structure, consisting of tightly synchronized logical and physical layers. The physical layer semantically corresponds to the SQL modeling / diagramming / generation functionality in MagicDraw. The logical layer corresponds to ER diagrams implemented by the Cameo Data Modeler Plugin.

The import functionality only imports logical layer data from ERwin into ER diagrams / data models in MagicDraw. The Cameo Data Modeler Plugin does not yet support importing physical layer data.

##### Importing data models

Cameo Data Modeler supports model files produced in ERwin version 7.x. It is recommended that you use the newest v7.3 since it has been heavily tested. Data models in ERwin must be saved in the *.xml format (choose the **XML Standard File**option in the **Save As**dialog).

To import an ERwin model

1. Start MagicDraw.
2. Click File > Import From > CA ERwin Data Modeler v7.x . The Open file dialog will open.
3. Select an ERwin model file (*.xml). A new MagicDraw project will be created and a logical model will be imported from the ERwin model file into that project.

After a successful import, you can proceed to edit or manage the model using MagicDraw features.

If you want to include the ER model as part of a larger project in MagicDraw, you can use either module linking functionality (click **File**> **Use Module**) to attach the ER model to your main project model or project import functionality (click **File**> **Import From**> **Another MagicDraw Project**) to transfer the contents of this ER model to your main project model.

If you want to update an imported and edited ER model (for example, you made changes to the ERwin model and want to import those changes into MagicDraw again), you can use the merge functionality (click **Tools**> **Project Merge**) to import the ERwin model into a new ER model and merge it with the model you have imported earlier.

##### Imported elements

Import mapping reviews and notes are in the following table.

| ERwin | Cameo Data Modeler | Comments |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Any element | Any Element | For each element, its name, definition, and notes are imported.Definitions are imported as MagicDraw documentation (special UML comments). Notes are imported as UML comments. |
| Entity | Entity |  |
| Attribute | Attribute | The Null / Not Null setting is imported as UML multiplicities [0..1] / [1].Attribute constraints and default value information is imported.Domain information is not imported because domains are not supported.Attribute type information is imported - the standard primitive types are mapped to the UML primitive types.Other types (those not found in the model) are created on the fly. |
| Key | Key Marking on Attributes | There is no separate standalone model element for a key in the Cameo Data Modeler ER diagrams. Instead, attributes belonging to a key are marked by applying a stereotype to them (PK, AK, or IE) as necessary. |
| Relationship | Association relationship | Simple relationships are mapped to UML associations.Verb phrases are mapped to role names.Cardinality and null / not null settings are mapped to UML multiplicities ([0..1], [1], [0..*], [1..*]).Referential integrity information is stored in a special stereotype / tag.Key information is not imported since the current ER diagrams do not support FK modeling. |
|  | Generalization relationship | ERwin relationships participating in the generalization tree are mapped to UML generalizations.Generalizations are joined into generalization trees.Complete / incomplete and overlapping / exclusive settings are imported / supported.Discriminating columns are imported / supported.Referential integrity information is stored in a special stereotype / tag.Verb phrase information is not imported. |
| Default Value | Instance Specification | A standalone UML instance specification is created to hold value definition. This instance specification is (or can be) then referenced from attributes, default value fields. |
| Domain | - | Domains are not yet supported in Cameo Data Modeler. |
| Validation Rule | Constraint | The Validation rule is stored as constraint body text. |
| Display | ER diagram | Due to geometric constraints and element size changes, the diagram layout will be slightly different.Paths between elements can be re-routed. |
| User Defined Properties Dictionary | Profile / Stereotypes / Tags | A custom UML profile is created for the user's property definitions. |
| User Defined Properties | Tag Values | A custom profile generated from the UDP dictionary is applied and user property information is stored in the tag values of the applied custom stereotypes. |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3bdfb118-d506-482e-a0e9-cf99e81ec16b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1276634702</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3f767494-e1fd-4dd3-a28e-d74ed34952ee"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1276634704</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="555e79f6-6ced-416e-9dc0-68acb862e5f8"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1276634701</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8876e9ef-4433-42eb-9a28-13b57941d9c9"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">3</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="minLevel">3</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="084383ad-ae67-4fbd-81a6-c1d7e6ae46ad"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1276634703</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The Cameo Data Modeler Plugin for MagicDraw provides import functionality for data models created using the CA ERwin® Data Modeler (henceforth will be referred as ERwin). It is recommended that you use the newest v7.3 since it has been heavily tested. Data models in ERwin must be saved in the *.xml format (choose the <strong>XML Standard File </strong>option in the <strong>Save As </strong>dialog).</p><p><br /></p><p>To import an ERwin model</p><hr /><ol><li>Start MagicDraw.</li><li>Click <strong>File </strong>&gt; <strong>Import From </strong>&gt; <strong>CA ERwin Data Modeler v7.x</strong>. A new MagicDraw project will be created and a logical model will be imported from the ERwin model file into that project.</li></ol><p>After a successful import, you can proceed to edit or manage the model using MagicDraw features.</p><p>If you want to include the ER model as part of a larger project in MagicDraw, you can use either module linking functionality (click <strong>File </strong>&gt; <strong>Use Module</strong>) to attach the ER model to your main project model or project import functionality (click <strong>File </strong>&gt; <strong>Import From </strong>&gt; <strong>Another MagicDraw Project</strong>) to transfer the contents of this ER model to your main project model.</p><p>If you want to update an imported and edited ER model (for example, you made changes to the ERwin model and want to import those changes into MagicDraw again), you can use the merge functionality (click <strong>Tools </strong>&gt; <strong>Project Merge</strong>) to import the ERwin model into a new ER model and merge it with the model you have imported earlier.</p><p><br /></p><h3>Imported elements</h3><p>Import mapping reviews and notes are in the following table.</p><table class="relative-table wrapped" style="width: 99.9451%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 13.4148%;" /><col style="width: 10.7423%;" /><col style="width: 75.8429%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th><strong>ERwin</strong></th><th><p><strong>Cameo Data Modeler</strong></p></th><th>Comments</th></tr><tr><td>Any element</td><td>Any Element</td><td><ul><li>For each element, its name, definition, and notes are imported.</li><li>Definitions are imported as MagicDraw documentation (special UML comments). Notes are imported as UML comments.</li></ul></td></tr><tr><td>Entity</td><td>Entity</td><td><br /></td></tr><tr><td>Attribute</td><td>Attribute</td><td><ul><li>The <strong>Null </strong>/ <strong>Not Null </strong>setting is imported as UML multiplicities [0..1] / [1].</li><li>Attribute constraints and default value information is imported.</li><li>Domain information is not imported because domains are not supported.</li><li>Attribute type information is imported - the standard primitive types are mapped to the UML primitive types.</li><li>Other types (those not found in the model) are created on the fly.</li></ul></td></tr><tr><td>Key</td><td><p>Key Marking on Attributes</p></td><td><ul><li>There is no separate standalone model element for a key in the Cameo Data Modeler ER diagrams. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243973852 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05494: Importing custom Jython libraries

- page_id: `243973852`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243973852/Importing+custom+Jython+libraries
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T19:18:22.614+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Macro engine > Supported scripting languages
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

It is possible to import custom Python libraries.

To import and use a custom *.py* file

1. Create a Lib directory in the <tool_installation_directory>\plugins\com.nomagic.magicdraw.automaton\lib\engines\jython directory.
2. Add the custom .py file to the Lib directory.
3. To use the custom *.py* script, execute the following (e.g. *myscript.py* is the name of the *.py* file):

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>It is possible to import custom Python libraries.</p><p>To import and use a custom <em>.py</em> file</p><hr /><ol><li>Create a <em>Lib</em> directory in the <em>&lt;tool_installation_directory&gt;\plugins\com.nomagic.magicdraw.automaton\lib\engines\jython</em> directory.</li><li>Add the custom <em>.py</em> file to the <em>Lib</em> directory.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">To use the custom <em>.py</em> script, execute the following (e.g. <em>myscript.py</em> is the name of the <em>.py</em> file):</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="114a7d2c-f334-462d-a92c-d86e3afd2bb9"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[import myscript
myscript.calculate(1, 5)]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243971400 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05495: Importing custom tool to template

- page_id: `243971400`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243971400/Importing+custom+tool+to+template
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:52:13.446+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Report Wizard > Appendix A: report extensions
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Report Wizard uses the import directive for importing a Custom Tool to a template.

An example of a directive:

```text

```

The import directive declares that the template uses the custom tool, names the tool that defines it, and specifies its tag prefix before the custom tool is used in a template page. You can use more than one import directive in a template, but the prefix defined in each template page must be unique.

#### Attributes

**name** (type:String) 
 The uniform or full qualified class name that locates the Tool class, for example, mytool.HelloTool

**prefix** (type:String) 
 The prefix that precedes the custom tool name such as a string in $hello.getHello(). Empty prefixes are not allowed. When developing or using custom tools, do not use tag prefixes such as bookmark, sorter, template, file, array, group, map, iterator, list, date, report, exporter, profiling, and project, as they are reserved by Report Wizard.

The code fragment indicated below declares a custom tool.

```text

```

The output from the above template is:

```text

```

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Report Wizard uses the import directive for importing a Custom Tool to a template.</p><p>An example of a directive:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="dd50b953-d474-470e-a26b-45c8eefbac29"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#import('prefix', 'name')]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The import directive declares that the template uses the custom tool, names the tool that defines it, and specifies its tag prefix before the custom tool is used in a template page. You can use more than one import directive in a template, but the prefix defined in each template page must be unique.</p><h2>Attributes</h2><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><strong>name</strong> (type:String)<br />            The uniform or full qualified class name that locates the Tool class, for example, mytool.HelloTool</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><strong>prefix</strong> (type:String)<br />            The prefix that precedes the custom tool name such as a string in $hello.getHello(). Empty prefixes are not allowed. When developing or using custom tools, do              not use tag prefixes such as bookmark, sorter, template, file, array, group, map, iterator, list, date, report, exporter, profiling, and project, as they are reserved                by Report Wizard.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">The code fragment indicated below declares a custom tool.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8a6f2762-f7a5-40ea-9382-07cadf641971"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#import('hello', 'mytool.HelloTool')
Get Hello
$hello.getHello()
Get Hello Foo
$hello.getHello('foo')]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The output from the above template is:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cfe379ac-2d2c-4a4a-98ff-bd3a4fbdaf5b"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Get Hello
Hello World
Get Hello Foo
Hello foo]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243968121 space=MT version=11 -->
## PAGE 05496: Importing data from Excel and CSV files

- page_id: `243968121`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243968121/Importing+data+from+Excel+and+CSV+files
- version_number: 11
- version_date: `2026-02-24T08:25:14.101+01:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Working with model elements
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can import data from Excel (*.xls*and*.xlsx*) or*CSV*(*.csv*and*.txt*) format files to your model. You are able to specify the import mapping options between file columns and element properties of the model. Additionally, you can save your maps, collect them in groups, manage those groups, and use an entire group instead of a single map for data import. Before importing data from Excel or CSV files to model, create new or open an existing project.

You can choose the following import commands:

- 
  - Import Using New Map - to import data from Excel or CSV file by specifying new mapping options and/or save an Import Map. >]]> [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Saving an Import Map' space='']>]]>
  - Import Using Existing Map - to import data from an Excel or CSV file by selecting a previously saved map. >]]> [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Saving an Import Map' space='']>]]>
  - **Import Using Existing Map Group** - to import data from Excel or CSV file by selecting the map group that collects as many Import Maps as you added. 
>]]>
  - Manage Map Groups - to create, delete or change the order of the m ap groups. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing Map Groups' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### All Excel/CSV file import commands from the File menu.

##### Preparing Excel or CSV file to import

To import data from Excel or CSV files, the files must meet the following prerequisites:

- Heading is located in the first row.
- When creating relationships and connectors, the spreadsheet must contain the source and target columns. The source and target columns contains the names/qualified names of elements respectively that are:
  - the client and supplier of the relationship.
  - the role (A) and role (B) for the connector ends. To import nested connectors, make sure to define each nested element in a new line of the same Excel table cell. This is later used for the qualified name construction, allowing the tool to accurately construct the connector ends.[ATTACHMENT filename='Nested Connector Import.png'][IMAGE alt='' src='']
- When creating connectors, the spreadsheet may optionally contain the owner column. The owner of the connector is determined in the following ways:
  - If the owner property is mapped, the connector is imported to the mapped owner.
  - If there is no owner property, the connector is imported to the element that owns the source and target elements indicated in the file.
    - If no such element exists in the model, the connector is imported to the owner of the element that is indicated as the source in the file.
  - If no qualified owner is found, the element selection dialog opens to select the owner for the connector.

Recommended prerequisites:

- Eliminate merged cells.
- Every column has a unique column name.
- Every row has the same number and name of columns.
- Every cell has the element name or a qualified element name.

##### Importing data by using a new map

To import data from an Excel or CSV file to model by using a new map

1. Create new or open an existing project.
2. Make sure:
  - the auto usages of Excel Import Plugin are removed. >]]>
  - the Excel or CSV file met the basic prerequisites. >]]>
3. In the top-left corner of the modeling tool, c lick the File > Import From > Excel/CSV File > Import Using New Map . The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Excel and CSV Import dialog' space='']**Excel/CSV Import** dialog opens.
4. Select the file and specify mapping options. [How to >>](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Excel+and+CSV+Import+dialog#ExcelandCSVImportdialog-SelectExcel/CSVfilearea) You must select the file in the **Select Excel/CSV file** areaand specify the**Element Type**,**Properties to Map,**and **Target Scope** mapping option values to import data.
5. In the Mapping area, create mapping between the Excel/CSV file data and modeling tool properties. [How to >>](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Excel+and+CSV+Import+dialog#ExcelandCSVImportdialog-Mappingarea)
6. (Optional) Click the Save Map button to save your map options as the Import Map . [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Saving an Import Map' space='']>]]>
7. Click the Import button. The data from the Excel or CSV file is imported to the model according to the specified options.

##### Importing data by using an existing map

To import data from Excel or CSV file to model by using an existing map

1. Create new or open an existing project.
2. Make sure:
  - the auto usages of Excel Import Plugin are removed. >]]>
  - the Excel or CSV file met the basic prerequisites. >]]>
3. In the top-left corner of the modeling tool, c lick the File > Import From > Excel/CSV File > Import Using Existing Map .
4. In the **Select Import Map** dialog, select existing Import Map. If you cannot see any Import Maps, you need to save it first. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Saving an Import Map' space='']>]]>
5. Click OK . The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Excel and CSV Import dialog' space='']**Excel/CSV Import** dialog opens.
6. (Optional) Select the file, change mapping options. [How to >>](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Excel+and+CSV+Import+dialog#ExcelandCSVImportdialog-SelectExcel/CSVfilearea)
7. (Optional) Click the Save Map button to override the selected Import Map or create a new one. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Saving an Import Map' space='']>]]>
8. Click the Import button. The data from the Excel or CSV file is imported to the model according to the specified options.

##### Importing data by using an existing map group

To import data from an Excel or CSV file to model by using an existing map group

1. Create new or open an existing project.
2. Make sure:
  - the auto usages of Excel Import Plugin are removed. >]]>
  - the Excel or CSV file met the basic prerequisites. >]]>
3. In the top-left corner of the modeling tool, c lick the File > Import From > Excel/CSV File > Import Using Existing Map Group .
4. In the **Select Map Group** dialog, choose the map group. If you cannot see any map group, you need to create it first. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing Map Groups' space='']>]]>
5. Click **OK**. 
The data from Excel or CSV files are imported to the model according to Import Maps and their order in the Map Group. The import of a map group can fail because of the following reasons:the Excel or CSV file is missing;the element type does not exist in the model;properties do not exist in the selected element;the target scope does not exist in the model.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

first. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Managing Map Groups" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[How to >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li data-uuid="0b44b0b0-9250-4101-be1e-529cde490e69"><p class="auto-cursor-target">Click <strong>OK</strong>.<br />The data from Excel or CSV files are imported to the model according to Import Maps and their order in the Map Group.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e8b55090-d00b-494c-b107-4c36ef33b9f0"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The import of a map group can fail because of the following reasons:</p><ul><li>the Excel or CSV file is missing;</li><li>the element type does not exist in the model;</li><li>properties do not exist in the selected element;</li><li>the target scope does not exist in the model.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243965891 space=MT version=3 -->
## PAGE 05497: Importing data from other UML tools and formats

- page_id: `243965891`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243965891/Importing+data+from+other+UML+tools+and+formats
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-08-07T18:06:38.435+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Working with projects
- labels: ['importing-uml', 'importing-data', 'msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

If you need to open a model of a different format or created with another UML tool, you have to import it to the modeling tool instead of simply opening it. The modeling tool allows importing projects from the following file formats:

- Another .mdzip project
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Excel and CSV Import dialog' space='']
- UML 2.1/2.5 XMI File
- MagicDraw Native XML File
- MOF XMI File
- CA ERwin Data Modeler v7.x
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Importing ReqIF files' space='']
- OWL Ontology File
- Eclipse UML2 (v2.x, v3.x, v4.x, v5.x) XMI file
- XPDL File
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='FMU import' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Simulink import' space='']
- Modelica Library

- Enterprise Architect UML 2.1 XMI 2.1 File. Data can be imported from an Enterprise Architect 7.1, 7.5, or 8.0 file. Please note that Enterprise Architect does not export 100% standard UML 2.1 XMI, and this causes some data loss during the import.

- Use[Sodius](https://www.sodiuswillert.com/en/home)to import data from the following products:
  - Publisher for Rational Software Architect
  - Publisher for System Architect
  - Publisher for Rhapsody

- UML diagrams from Microsoft Visio using XMI files. Microsoft Visio 2007 and earlier requires extra add-ons for exporting UML model to XMI files.

To import an external model

1. Select File > **Import** From .
2. Select a file format you want to import a model from.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

Data can be imported from an Enterprise Architect 7.1, 7.5, or 8.0 file.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5e76de8f-6f1d-46d0-a283-b9757cf03d8c"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Please note that Enterprise Architect does not export 100% standard UML 2.1 XMI, and this causes some data loss during the import.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ul><ul><li><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Use </span><a href="https://www.sodiuswillert.com/en/home">Sodius</a><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> to import data from the following products:</span><br /></span><ul><li>Publisher for Rational Software Architect</li><li><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Publisher for System Architect</span></p></li><li><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Publisher for Rhapsody</span></p></li></ul></li></ul><ul><li><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">UML diagrams from Microsoft Visio using XMI files.<br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="55b087f9-d2c1-461d-b087-fd7b5f19607d"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Microsoft Visio 2007 and earlier requires extra add-ons for exporting UML model to XMI files.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ul><p><br /></p><p>To import an external model</p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Select </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">File</strong><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> &gt; </span><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><strong>Import</strong></span><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">From</strong>.</li><li>Select a file format you want to import a model from. </li></ol>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243965906 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05498: Importing Diagrams

- page_id: `243965906`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243965906/Importing+Diagrams
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:49:48.898+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Working with projects > Importing data from other UML tools and formats > Enterprise Architect Import
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Enterprise Architect Import allows you to import diagrams. The imported diagram information includes:

33

##### Geometry Properties

The geometry properties imported to a UML model are:

- Positions on a diagram (for shape elements)
- Width and height (for shape elements)
- Path break points (for link elements)

#### NOTE: Note

Note

Other display properties can override geometry information. For example, if an imported element width is shorter than the required width to display text on the element, the width will be adjusted automatically.

##### Color Properties

Color properties will be imported along with the diagrams to a modeling tool developed by NoMagic. Each color property has a different name in a modeling tool. The following table shows color properties mapping.

| EA | Modeling tool by NoMagic |
| --- | --- |
| Background | Fill color |
| Border color (for shape element) | Pen color |
| Font color | Text color |
| Line color (for link element) | Pen color |

##### Display Properties

Display properties in EA can be categorized into three groups: shape, link, and diagram. Only those properties corresponding to the modeling tool will be imported. For example, the **Show Diagram Details**property in EA will be imported as the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Table with diagram information' space='']**Show Diagram Info**property in a modeling tool developed by NoMagic.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

Each color property has a different name in a modeling tool. The following table shows color properties mapping.</p><table class="relative-table wrapped" style="width: 32.1145%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 64.0199%;" /><col style="width: 35.9801%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>EA</th><th><span>Modeling tool by NoMagic</span></th></tr><tr><td>Background</td><td><p>Fill color</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Border color (for shape element)</p></td><td><p>Pen color</p></td></tr><tr><td><span>Font<span> </span>color</span></td><td><p>Text color</p></td></tr><tr><td><span>Line color (for link element)</span></td><td><p>Pen color</p></td></tr></tbody></table><h3>Display Properties</h3><p>Display properties in EA can be categorized into three groups: shape, link, and diagram. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249565178 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05500: Importing from ArchiMate

- page_id: `249565178`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249565178/Importing+from+ArchiMate
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-09-11T12:59:56.251+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > Procedures
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Introduction to ArchiMate

The ArchiMate language is an open and independent modeling language for Enterprise Architecture that enables Enterprise Architects to describe, analyze, and visualize the relationships among architecture domains in an unambiguous way. The ArchiMate supports an exchange format in XML, which allows model and diagram exchange between tools.

https://publications.opengroup.org/standards/archimate

##### Importing ArchiMate model from open exchange file

You can import ArchiMate model to your UAF project. The supported file format for importing is ArchiMate *.xml*.

To import the model from ArchiMate .xml file

1. In the main menu, click File > Import From > ArchiMate XML File .
2. In the open dialog, choose the ArchiMate .xml file you want to import.
3. Click Open .
4. In the Select Owner dialog, choose or create the owner and click OK .

After the import is completed, the notification is displayed with the import results. The imported ArchiMate elements are converted to the UAF elements and added to your model. You can find the imported elements in the Containment tree. How ArchiMate model elements and relationships map to the UAF elements described in the following pages:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Element mapping' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Relationship mapping' space='']

Also, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Mapping issues' space=''] chapter to learn about known mapping issues.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<h3>Introduction to ArchiMate</h3><p>The ArchiMate language is an open and independent modeling language for Enterprise Architecture that enables Enterprise Architects to describe, analyze, and visualize the relationships among architecture domains in an unambiguous way. The ArchiMate supports an exchange format in XML, which allows model and diagram exchange between tools.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5e997923-3556-47be-aa3e-91a4a374d395"><ac:rich-text-body>For more information, see <a href="https://publications.opengroup.org/standards/archimate">https://publications.opengroup.org/standards/archimate</a>.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Importing ArchiMate model from open exchange file</h3><p>You can import ArchiMate model to your UAF project. The supported file format for importing is ArchiMate <em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">.xml</em>.</p><p><br /></p><p>To import the model from ArchiMate .xml file</p><hr /><ol><li>In the main menu, click <strong>File</strong> &gt; <strong>Import From</strong> &gt; <strong>ArchiMate XML File</strong>.</li><li>In the open dialog, choose the ArchiMate<em> .xml</em> file you want to import.</li><li>Click <strong>Open</strong>.</li><li>In the <strong>Select Owner</strong> dialog, choose or create the owner and click <strong>OK</strong>.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>After the import is completed, the notification is displayed with the import results. The imported ArchiMate elements are converted to the UAF elements and added to your model. You can find the imported elements in the Containment tree. How ArchiMate model elements and relationships map to the UAF elements described in the following pages:</p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Element mapping" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Relationship mapping" /></ac:link></li></ul><p>Also, see the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Mapping issues" /></ac:link> chapter to learn about known mapping issues.</p><p><span><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="1044"><ri:attachment ri:filename="ArchiMate 3.1 to UAF 1.2 horizontal-white-background copy 2.jpg" /></ac:image><br /></span></p><p><span><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="625"><ri:attachment ri:filename="image2022-11-3_14-59-16.png" /></ac:image><br /></span></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243968170 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05501: Importing instances

- page_id: `243968170`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243968170/Importing+instances
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-07T17:23:09.133+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Working with model elements > Importing data from Excel and CSV files > Import examples
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Lets say, we need to import the nested instances with slots in the already prepared Engine model structure where the *Engine* is decomposed to *Engine Type*, as shown in the figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The *Engine* Block will be a classifier for three engine instances. Each engine instance will contain *serialNum*, *year*slots with defined values and nested *engine Type* instances*.* Each nested *engine Type* instances will contain four *fuel*, *hp*, *make* and *model* slots with defined values. In the following figure, the Excel spreadsheet is prepared for the instances import. [Learn more how to prepare the Excel or CSV file for import >>](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Importing+data+from+Excel+or+CSV+files#ImportingdatafromExcelorCSVfiles-PreparingExcelorCSVfiletoimport)

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

Next, begin the import: in the top-left corner of the modeling tool, click the**File**>**Import From**>**Excel/CSV File > Import Using New Map**. In the [**Excel/CSV Import** dialog](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Excel+and+CSV+Import+dialog) specify options:

1. Select the Excel file which contains the spreadsheet with instances. In this case, the Vehicle model import Excel file is selected.
2. Select Instances as Import Type option value.
3. Select Engine Block as Instance Classifier option value.
4. Select Value Properties of the Engine Block and engine Type Part Property as the Nested Properties To Map option values. [ATTACHMENT filename='select_nested_properties.png']
5. Specify the location of imported elements in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Containment Tree' space='MED'] by selecting the Target Scope option value. In this case, the Instances Package is selected.
6. Specify the Excel file sheet in which the table is prepared to import. In this case, the table is in the Sheet 4.
7. Create mapping between model properties and excel table cells.
8. Click Import . [ATTACHMENT filename='instances_import_dialog_options.png']

As a result, three nested engine instances and their slots are created with defined values.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

Each engine instance will contain <em>serialNum</em>, <em>year </em>slots with defined values and nested <em>engine Type</em> instances<em>.</em> Each nested <em>engine Type</em> instances will contain four <em>fuel</em>, <em>hp</em>, <em>make</em> and <em>model</em> slots with defined values. In the following figure, the Excel spreadsheet is prepared for the instances import. <a style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;" href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Importing+data+from+Excel+or+CSV+files#ImportingdatafromExcelorCSVfiles-PreparingExcelorCSVfiletoimport">Learn more how to prepare the Excel or CSV file for import &gt;&gt;</a></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="engines_excel_spreadsheet.png" /></ac:image></p><p>Next, begin the import: i<span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">n the top-left corner of the modeling tool, c</span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">lick the </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">File</strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> &gt; </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Import From</strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> &gt; </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Excel/CSV File &gt; Import Using New Map</strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">. In t</span>he <a style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;" href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Excel+and+CSV+Import+dialog"><strong>Excel/CSV Import</strong> dialog</a> specify options:</p><ol><li>Select the Excel file which contains the spreadsheet with instances. In this case, the <em>Vehicle model import</em> Excel file is selected.</li><li>Select Instances as <strong>Import Type</strong> option value.</li><li>Select <em>Engine</em> Block as <strong>Instance Classifier</strong> option value.</li><li>Select Value Properties of the <em>Engine</em> Block and <em>engine Type</em> Part Property as the <strong>Nested Properties To Map</strong> option values.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="select_nested_properties.png" /></ac:image></li><li>Specify the location of imported elements in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Containment Tree" /></ac:link> by selecting the<strong> Target Scope</strong> option value. In this case, the <em>Instances </em>Package is selected.</li><li>Specify the Excel file sheet in which the table is prepared to import. In this case, the table is in the Sheet 4.</li><li>Create mapping between model properties and excel table cells.</li><li>Click <strong>Import</strong>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="instances_import_dialog_options.png" /></ac:image></li></ol><p>As a result, three nested engine instances and their slots are created with defined values. </p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="imported_instances.png" /></ac:image></p><p><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243966144 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05502: Importing models

- page_id: `243966144`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243966144/Importing+models
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:49:53.875+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Working with projects > Project partitioning > Composing model
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Importing models from used projects

If you no longer need to have the set of elements in a separate project, you can import these elements into the main project.

To import a used project into the main project

1. In the Model Browser, select the package of this used project and open the shortcut menu .
2. Select Project Usages > Import .

All the elements of this used project are copied into the main project, and links to this used project are removed from the main project. The unlinked project and its elements continue to exist in the file system or server after the import.

- If the used project you want to import uses other projects, after the import these projects become directly used in the main project.
- The Standard/System Profiles cannot be imported according to our tool policy.

##### Importing models from other projects

Another way of composing your model, is to import a model from another project.

To import a model from another project

1. In the main menu, select File > Import From > Another Project .
2. Select a project you want to import.
3. Click **Import**. All elements from another project will be imported. If the model that you are importing also uses projects, these projects will be reused directly in your project after the model import.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<h3>Importing models from used projects</h3><p>If you no longer need to have the set of elements in a separate project, you can import these elements into the main project.</p><p> </p><p>To import a used project into the main project</p><hr /><ol><li>In the Model Browser, select the package of this used project and <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="cd108d97-84da-4865-8ccf-44b486b6e0c6">open the shortcut menu</ac:inline-comment-marker>.</li><li>Select <strong>Project Usages</strong> &gt; <strong>Import</strong>.</li></ol><p>All the elements of this used project are copied into the main project, and links to this used project are removed from the main project. The unlinked project and its elements continue to exist in the file system or server after the import.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4790239a-deb3-4f59-ba25-1ce2e1cdf086"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>If the used project you want to import uses other projects, after the import these projects become directly used in the main project.</li><li>The Standard/System Profiles cannot be imported according to our tool policy.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3 class="with-breadcrumbs">Importing models from other projects</h3><p>Another way of composing your model, is to import a model from another project.</p><p> </p><p>To import a model from another project</p><hr /><ol><li>In the main menu, select <strong>File</strong> &gt; <strong>Import From</strong> &gt; <strong>Another Project</strong>.</li><li>Select a project you want to import.</li><li><p>Click <strong>Import</strong>. All elements from another project will be imported.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b8f81a64-5f1e-4400-a66f-28ac9ff8134f"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If the model that you are importing also uses projects, these projects will be reused directly in your project after the model import.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243968157 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05503: Importing nested Requirements

- page_id: `243968157`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243968157/Importing+nested+Requirements
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-07T17:23:07.443+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Working with model elements > Importing data from Excel and CSV files > Import examples
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Lets' say we need to import nested structure of requirements from the Excel file. For this, you must define the Owner property in the Excel spreadsheet with the name or id of each requirement. As example, see the following figure.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Requirements in the Excel spreadsheet with defined Owner property.

To import nested Requirements

1. Open the project in the modeling tool.
2. In the top-left corner of the modeling tool, click the **File** > **Import From** > **Excel/CSV File > Import Using New Map**. For element import via tables, use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sync with Excel or CSV files' space=''] functionality.
3. In the [**Excel/CSV Import** dialog](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Excel+and+CSV+Import+dialog), select the Excel file. Make sure the Excel file contains Owner column. See the example above.
4. Specify the mapping options:
  - Import Type - select the Element value.
  - Element Type - select the Requirement type you want to create.
  - Properties to Map - select Name, Text, id and Owner properties you want to fill in with data from Excel file.
  - **Target Scope**- select the Package you want to contain imported Requirements. If the Owner property is selected as the **Properties to Map** option value, the data is imported according the owner. You can specify more mapping options according to your needs. [Learn more about mapping****options >>](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Excel+and+CSV+Import+dialog#ExcelandCSVImportdialog-MappingOptionsarea)
5. Create the mapping between Excel file columns and element properties by dragging the column on the right to the property on the left. [Learn more about mapping area >>](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Excel+and+CSV+Import+dialog#ExcelandCSVImportdialog-Mappingarea)
6. (Optional) Click the Save Map button to save your map options as the Import Map. Learn more how to save Import Map >>
7. Click the Import button. The Requirements are imported. If any of the imported Requirement already exist in the model t hey are updated. To avoid duplication of elements specify **Identification Property** value or **I****dentify Relationship (End) Source by** and **Identify Relationship (End) Target by** values. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Excel and CSV Import dialog' space='']>]]>

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

See the example above.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li>Specify the mapping options:<br /><ul><li><strong>Import Type </strong>- select the Element value.</li><li><strong>Element Type </strong>- select the Requirement type you want to create.</li><li><strong>Properties to Map</strong> - select Name, Text, id and Owner properties you want to fill in with data from Excel file. </li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Target Scope </strong>- select the Package you want to contain imported Requirements. <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">If the Owner property is selected as the <strong>Properties to Map</strong> option value, the data is imported according the owner.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="980f374f-bd9c-4ec1-aa28-1eccd62e0348"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> You can specify more mapping options according to your needs. <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Excel+and+CSV+Import+dialog#ExcelandCSVImportdialog-MappingOptionsarea">Learn more about mapping<strong> </strong>options &gt;&gt;</a></span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ul></li><li><p>Create the mapping between Excel file columns and element properties by dragging the column on the right to the property on the left. <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Excel+and+CSV+Import+dialog#ExcelandCSVImportdialog-Mappingarea"> Learn more about mapping area &gt;&gt;</a></p></li><li>(Optional) Click the<strong> Save Map</strong> button to save your map options as the Import Map. <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Saving+an+Import+Map">Learn more how to save Import Map &gt;&gt;</a></li><li>Click the <strong>Import</strong><span> button.<br /></span>The Requirements are imported. If any of the imported Requirement already exist in the model t<span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">hey are updated. To avoid duplication of elements specify <strong>Identification Property</strong> value</span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> or <strong>I</strong><span><strong>dentify Relationship (End) Source by</strong> and <strong><span>I</span><span>dentify Relationship (End) Target by</span></strong> values. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Excel and CSV Import dialog" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more about mapping options in the Excel/CSV Import dialog>>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span></span></li></ol><p><ac:image ac:align="left"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Importing_nested_req.png" /></ac:image></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243966221 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05504: Importing projects from Ecore files

- page_id: `243966221`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243966221/Importing+projects+from+Ecore+files
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-07T17:22:29.075+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Working with projects > Ecore support
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

There is no possibility to import Ecore files directly. Ecore files should be imported using the EMOF import feature. 
 
To import an Ecore file

1. In the Eclipse environment, open the *.ecore file and save it as *.emof.
2. Use the Importing projects from MOF files procedure.

This way of importing Ecore files may cause some Ecore-specific model details that don't exist in EMOF to be lost.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>There is no possibility to import Ecore files directly. Ecore files should be imported using the EMOF import feature. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> To import an Ecore file</span></p><hr /><ol><li>In the Eclipse environment, open the *.ecore file and save it as *.emof.</li><li>Use the <a href="http://documentation.nomagic.com/display/MT/Importing+projects+from+MOF+files">Importing projects from MOF files</a> procedure.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="32276341-4f32-43be-be16-e7105068a796"><ac:rich-text-body><p>This way of importing Ecore files may cause some Ecore-specific model details that don't exist in EMOF to be lost.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243966199 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 05505: Importing projects from MOF files

- page_id: `243966199`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243966199/Importing+projects+from+MOF+files
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:49:55.366+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Working with projects > MOF support
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To import a project from a MOF file

1. From the main menu, select File > Import From > MOF XMI File .
2. In the dialog, select the EMOF or CMOF file you want to import, and click Open . The file is imported as a separate project.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>To import a project from a MOF file</p><hr /><ol><li>From the main menu, select <strong>File</strong> &gt; <strong>Import From</strong> &gt; <strong>MOF XMI File</strong>.</li><li>In the dialog, select the EMOF or CMOF file you want to import, and click <strong>Open</strong>. The file is imported as a separate project.</li></ol><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243968166 space=MT version=4 -->
## PAGE 05506: Importing relationships

- page_id: `243968166`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243968166/Importing+relationships
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-09-10T12:08:19.113+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Working with model elements > Importing data from Excel and CSV files > Import examples
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Main conditions

You can create relationships between existing model elements by importing them from Excel/CSV files. For thisthe following conditions must be met:

- 
  - The source/client and target/supplierelements you want to create relationships between must exist in the project.
  - The Excel/CSV source and target columns must be mapped with the Client/Source and Supplier/Target properties in the mapping area of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Excel and CSV Import dialog' space=''].
  - The source and target elements must be legal in terms of Metamodel, DSL and code rules, e.g., the satisfy relationship client must be a requirement.

##### Preparing the Excel file to import relationships

When creating relationships in the model by importing them, you need to make sure the Excel file contains the source and target columns. The source column contains the names of elements that are the client of the relationship. The target column contains the names of the elements that are the suppliers of the relationship. In the example below, the Source column contains the names of the Blocks and the Target column contains the names of Requirements.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Excel file content prepared with element names for the relationships import.

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Importing Blocks and their properties' space='']>]]>[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Importing nested Requirements' space='']>]]>

If you have to deal with a large a scope of information, the elements are not under the same target scope, or you want to increase the import speed, use the following:

- Select to search elements and references in the Target Scope Only in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Excel and CSV Import dialog' space='']**Excel/CSV Import** dialog when importing data.
- Define your source and target columns in the Excel file with qualified names of elements. In the example below, the Source column contains the qualified names of the Blocks and the Target column contains the qualified names of Requirements.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The The Excel file content prepared with qualified element names for the relationships import.

##### Relationships import procedure

To import relationships

1. Prepare the Excel file for relationships import. >]]>
2. Open the project in the modeling tool. Make sure the elements you want to create relationships between exist in the project. 
[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Importing Blocks and their properties' space='']>]]>[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Importing nested Requirements' space='']>]]>
3. In the top-left corner of the modeling tool, click the **File** > **Import From** > **Excel/CSV File > Import Using New Map**. For element import via tables, use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sync with Excel or CSV files' space=''] functionality.
4. In the **Excel/CSV Import** dialog , select the Excel file. How to >>
5. Specify the mapping options:
  - Import Type - select the Element value.
  - Element Type - select the relationship type you want to create.
  - Properties to Map - the Client and Supplier properties are selected by default.
  - **Target Scope**- select the Package you want to contain imported relationships. If the Owner property is selected as the **Properties to Map** option value, the data is imported according the owner. You can specify more mapping options according to your needs. [Learn more about mapping****options >>](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Excel+and+CSV+Import+dialog#ExcelandCSVImportdialog-MappingOptionsarea)
6. Create the mapping between Excel file columns and element properties by dragging the Source column on the right to the Client property on the left. Do the same with the Target and Supplier or other columns/properties.[Learn more about mapping area >>](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Excel+and+CSV+Import+dialog#ExcelandCSVImportdialog-Mappingarea)
7. (Optional) Click the Save Map button to save your map options as the Import Map. Learn more how to save Import Map >>
8. Click the Import button. The relationships are imported. If any of the imported relationships already exist in the model t hey are updated. To avoid duplication of elements specify **Identification Property** value or **I****dentify Relationship (End) Source by** and **Identify Relationship (End) Target by** values. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Excel and CSV Import dialog' space='']>]]>

The relationship import can fail due to the following reasons:

- The relationship end is empty in the data file.
- The source or the target elements do not exist in the model or the mechanism didn't find any.

In the following example, you can see specified import options in the **Excel/CSV Import** dialog before the import. After the import the Satisfy relationships are created between the defined Source and Target elements: Blocks and Requirements.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The import of Satisfy relationship.

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Importing data from Excel and CSV files' space='']
- Saving an Import Map
- Managing Map Groups
- Excel and CSV Import dialog
- [Sync with Excel or CSV files](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Sync+with+Excel+or+CSV+files)

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

For this </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">the following conditions must be met:</span></p><ul><li style="list-style-type: none;background-image: none;" data-uuid="c7da3f44-6383-4771-8cb9-42577aae5f46"><ul><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">The source/client and target/supplier </span>elements you want to create relationships between must exist in the project.</span><br /></span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">The Excel/CSV source and target columns must be mapped with the Client/Source and Supplier/Target properties in the mapping area of the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Excel and CSV Import dialog" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Excel/CSV Import dialog]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. </span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">The source and target elements must be legal in terms of Metamodel, DSL and code rules, e.g., the satisfy relationship client must be a requirement.</span></li></ul></li></ul><h3>Preparing the Excel file to import relationships</h3><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">When creating relationships in the model by importing them, you need to make sure the Excel file contains the source and target columns. Do the same with the Target and Supplier or other columns/properties.<a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Excel+and+CSV+Import+dialog#ExcelandCSVImportdialog-Mappingarea"> Learn more about mapping area &gt;&gt;</a></p></li><li data-uuid="c5eb9fbe-6204-48ff-a06f-5e1796c54566">(Optional) Click the<strong> Save Map</strong> button to save your map options as the Import Map. <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Saving+an+Import+Map">Learn more how to save Import Map &gt;&gt;</a></li><li data-uuid="a8ed08f1-e400-4986-ae34-ab8fcf8ca10d">Click the <strong>Import</strong><span> button.<br /></span>The relationships are imported. If any of the imported relationships already exist in the model t<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">hey are updated. To avoid duplication of elements specify <strong>Identification Property</strong> value</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> or <strong>I</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><strong>dentify Relationship (End) Source by</strong> and <strong>Identify Relationship (End) Target by</strong> values. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Excel and CSV Import dialog" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn more about mapping options in the Excel/CSV Import dialog>>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><br /></span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><br /></span></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="11b36b6e-52a8-475f-92cc-71a4f243e323"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">The relationship import can fail due to the following reasons:</span></p><ul><li data-uuid="6010d621-7113-41f8-908b-bf33cceb6169"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">The relationship end is empty in the data file.</span></li><li data-uuid="5c61116c-9957-4e70-8a90-6342160076b6"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">The source or the target elements do not exist in the model or the mechanism didn't find any.</span></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the following example, you can see specified import options in the <strong>Excel/CSV Import</strong> dialog before the import. After the import the Satisfy relationships are created between the defined Source and Target elements: Blocks and Requirements. </p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="result_of_relationships_import.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The import of Satisfy relationship.</h6></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li class="with-breadcrumbs" data-uuid="f11b0090-f4eb-4ef5-a455-f9c5f314c926"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Importing data from Excel and CSV files" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Importing data from Excel or CSV files]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li class="with-breadcrumbs" data-uuid="5e0575a7-8ec4-4394-8266-e065ee41472c"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Saving+an+Import+Map" class="current">Saving an Import Map</a></li><li class="with-breadcrumbs" data-uuid="1caecd53-5725-4691-bcb5-d963df23ba93"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Managing+Map+Groups" class="current">Managing Map Groups</a></li><li class="with-breadcrumbs" data-uuid="76b2d164-7b9c-4cdd-a8d9-6871a1773bbb"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Excel+and+CSV+Import+dialog" class="current">Excel and CSV Import dialog</a></li><li class="with-breadcrumbs" data-uuid="72458b2c-df78-4cb9-ae30-4925b52e3bf6"><p class="with-breadcrumbs"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/Sync+with+Excel+or+CSV+files" class="current">Sync with Excel or CSV files</a></p></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249005579 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 05507: Importing ReqIF files

- page_id: `249005579`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249005579/Importing+ReqIF+files
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T17:23:11.385+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > SysML v1 modeling > Requirements management > Importing requirements
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

818054109

INLINE

818054112

INLINE

818062608

INLINE

**On this page**

43

818054111

INLINE

##### Importing ReqIF files

You can import ReqIF files from:

- IBM DOORS 9.4, 9.5, and 9.6
- IBM DOORS Next Generation
- PTC Integrity
- Polarion REQUIREMENTS
- Siemens Teamcenter
- Dassault Systemes Reqtify

You can import any ReqIF file – with or without specifications.

To import a ReqIF requirement file

1. Open a project wherein you want to import requirements.
2. From the File menu, select Import From > Requirement Interchange Format (ReqIF) File .
3. In the opened **Load** dialog, browse for your ReqIF file and click **Open**.
4. The ReqIF file is imported.

If Requirements in the ReqIF file have ID prefixes, they are set automatically after import only for imported Requirements. Manage ID prefixes when [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Requirements numbering' space=''].

800700

When the ReqIF file is imported, a notification message appears, stating the quantity of imported or updated elements. If you import a ReqIF file without specification, the message doesn't contain the line that indicates new or updated specifications. You also see no option **Show imported specification table(s)**. 
 
If you imported a ReqIF file with the specification, you can view the list of imported elements. To do that, click the **Show imported specification table(s)** link in the opened notification message. The list is displayed in a generic table format.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To change import options, click the **You can change import and mapping options here** link in the opened notification message.

The notification message is displayed for a fixed duration, but you can find all project notification messages in the Notification Window. To open the Notification Window, click **Window** > **Notification Window**, or press Ctrl + M.

Imported requirements are presented in the **Containment** tree. The requirement specification is represented as a model package and requirements are represented as model elements. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

If there are images or files attached to the ReqIF file, they are marked with the «AttachedFile» stereotype and placed in a separate package as model elements after the import.

All images in the *BMP* file format are automatically converted to the *PNG*format.

If there are any deleted elements in the source file, they are automatically marked with the «deprecated» stereotype once imported onto the modeling tool.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Changing requirements importing options

Changing requirements importing options is beneficial if you have a custom requirement type defined, or you are importing another type of data instead of requirements, for example, test cases.

A requirement type is limited to class and class extended types.

You can change the following import options, among others:

- Specify an element type to create for the objects you are importing;
- Specify a relationship type to create for the relationships you are importing;
- Specify a path to the resource location;
- Choose whether to import all properties into the model or not and form a list of ignored and not imported properties;
- Choose which properties that, when modified, will not change the elements' status to 'updated';
- For a full list, see the 'To change ReqIF file importing options' procedure or the ReqIF section in the Project Options table on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Setting project options' space=''] table.

To change ReqIF file importing options

We recommend changing importing options before requirements are imported and no changes are made in the project, because after you have changed importing options, you need to remove the requirements you have imported and re-import it.

1. Click Options > Project . The Project Options dialog opens.
2. In the options group list, select General , then scroll down to the ReqIF options group.
3. Click a desired option specification cell to change it's value.
4. Click OK after you are finished.

##### Updating requirements

It is common to modify requirements after importing them. Importing requirements in a ReqIF format allows updating the requirements present in the model without any data loss – all changes are identified. 
 
To update ReqIF file by importing a new file

1. Open a project wherein you want to update requirements.
2. Import a ReqIF file with updated requirements.

To update requirements from the project

1. Open the project containing imported requirements.
2. In the Containment tree, select a requirement specification package and open it's shortcut menu.
3. Click Tools > ReqIF > Update From Requirements Interchange Format (ReqIF) File .
4. In the opened Load dialog, browse for your ReqIF file and click Open . The ReqIF file is updated.

After requirements have been updated, you get a notification message informing about an update status. If you update a ReqIF file without specification, the message doesn't contain the line that indicates new or updated specifications. You also see no option **Show imported specification table(s)**. 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
In the change log table, you can see the detailed list of updated elements. 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
During update, no elements are removed, only new elements are added and statuses of all elements are updated. Elements not present in an updated ReqIF file are marked as obsolete.

##### Managing obsolete requirement elements

In the model, obsolete elements are marked with an exclamation mark in a yellow triangle. A «deprecated» stereotype is applied to such requirement elements automatically. 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
**Removing Obsolete Elements** 
In the project, you can choose to remove only the selected obsolete element or all obsolete elements at a time. To remove one selected obsolete element, use the that is described in the following section. 
 
To remove all obsolete elements

1. In the Containment tree, right-click the requirements specification package.
2. From the shortcut menu, select Tools > ReqIF > Remove Obsolete Elements .

****

**Searching for obsolete elements**

#validationTo search for obsolete elements

1. In the Containment tree, right-click the requirements specification package.
2. From the shortcut menu, select Validation > Obsolete Elements and one of the following:

- 
  - Remove Obsolete Elements – removes the selected obsolete elements from a project
  - Ignore – ignores obsolescence from the element and leaves it in the model
  - Select in Validation Results – opens the Active Validation Results panel wherein you can perform appropriate validation actions.

818054108
